v.t: Cao Cao pursues victory after Guandu, gradually pacifies Hebei
v.s: In 200 AD, Cao Cao defeated the main forces of Yuan Shao at Guandu. By 201, Cao Cao crossed the Yellow River and attacked Cangting (modern Yanggu, Shandong), defeating Yuan Shao's forces again. Yuan Shao retreated to Yecheng and soon fell gravely ill. Cao Cao took control of most of the middle and lower Yellow River region, laying the foundation for unifying northern China.

v.t: Roman emperor Caracalla continues construction of the Baths of Caracalla
v.s: Roman emperor Caracalla completed the Baths of Caracalla in 212 AD, one of the grandest public baths of the Roman Empire. In 201 AD, construction was ongoing. Covering 13 hectares and accommodating 1,600 bathers at once, the complex included cold, warm, and hot baths, a library, gymnasium, and gardens, representing the peak of Roman architecture and engineering.

v.t: Indian Buddhism spreads to Southeast Asia via maritime trade routes
v.s: From the 2nd century AD, Indian Buddhist monks traveled with merchant ships to Southeast Asia, establishing monasteries in Burma, Thailand, Cambodia, etc. In the Kushan Empire, the Gandhara region (modern northern Pakistan) developed Buddhist iconography blending Greek artistic styles, which influenced all of East Asia as Buddhism spread.

v.t: Roman emperor Caracalla assassinated by Praetorian prefect Macrinus
v.s: During a campaign against Parthia, Roman emperor Caracalla was killed by assassins sent by his Praetorian prefect Macrinus near Carrhae (modern southern Turkey). Caracalla was known for his tyranny, having massacred thousands of Alexandrians and granted Roman citizenship to all free men (the Antonine Constitution of 212) to increase tax revenue. His death ended the Severan dynasty.

v.t: Cao Cao attacks Liyang; after Yuan Shao's death, his sons fall into infighting
v.s: Yuan Shao died of illness in Yecheng in summer 202. His sons Yuan Tan and Yuan Shang fought over succession. Cao Cao took the opportunity to march north and attack Liyang (modern Xun County, Henan). Yuan Shang led troops to relieve the siege but was defeated. Cao Cao did not storm Yecheng but sat back to watch the brothers fight, setting the stage for their eventual destruction.

v.t: Roman popular uprising forces Macrinus to reform commercial taxes
v.s: Macrinus succeeded Caracalla as Roman emperor, but as a knight-class emperor he commanded little respect from the Senate. To solve the fiscal crisis, he cut military pay, causing discontent among the soldiers; at the same time, he reformed the commercial tax system, raising tariffs, which provoked resistance from merchants in the eastern provinces.

v.t: Yuan Shao dies; Yuan Tan and Yuan Shang fight over succession
v.s: Yuan Shao, the overlord of Jizhou, fell ill and died in May 202 in Yecheng due to his crushing defeat at Guandu. He had favored his youngest son Yuan Shang and named him as heir, but the eldest son Yuan Tan refused to accept. Yuan Tan, allied with Yuan Xi and Gao Gan, opposed Yuan Shang. Yuan Tan stationed his troops at Liyang, and Yuan Shang sent reinforcements. The fraternal strife gave Cao Cao an opportunity.

v.t: Macrinus proclaimed Roman emperor by eastern army
v.s: After Caracalla's assassination, Macrinus became the first Roman emperor not of senatorial origin, backed by the army. He quickly made peace with Parthia, ending Caracalla's war, but this peace policy deeply disappointed the legionaries, who had hoped for plunder.

v.t: Cao Cao captures Liyang, gains full control north of the Yellow River
v.s: Taking advantage of the infighting between Yuan Tan and Yuan Shang, Cao Cao again marched north. Yuan Tan, unable to resist, begged Yuan Shang for help; Yuan Shang led troops to rescue him, and the two sides fought fiercely under the walls of Liyang. Cao Cao crushed the Yuan forces, and Yuan Tan and Yuan Shang fled to Yecheng. Cao Cao did not pursue but returned to Xuchang, and at the Han emperor's command appointed Yuan Tan as Inspector of Qingzhou, further driving a wedge between the Yuan brothers.

v.t: Egyptian Christian school – Catechetical School of Alexandria flourishes
v.s: Alexandria in Roman Egypt was not only a center of learning but also a major hub of Christian scholarship. The Catechetical School produced great theologians such as Clement and Origen (c.184–253). Origen wrote the Hexapla and On First Principles, blending Greek philosophy with Christian theology, influencing later Christian thought.

v.t: Roman emperor Severus reforms Praetorian Guard, expands recruitment of barbarians
v.s: To consolidate power, Roman emperor Septimius Severus disbanded the existing Praetorian Guard and formed a new one from elite soldiers of his Danubian legions, allowing soldiers to marry during service. He also recruited large numbers of Germans and Illyrians into the army, accelerating its barbarization.

v.t: Cao Cao captures Yecheng, the Yuan clan's stronghold, unifying Hebei
v.s: Taking advantage of Yuan Shang's campaign against Yuan Tan, Cao Cao personally led his army to besiege Yecheng (modern Linzhang, Hebei). After months of siege, the city ran out of food. Yuan Shang's relief force was routed, and the city's defender, Shen Pei, was captured and executed. After occupying Yecheng, Cao Cao issued a 'Call for Talent' and implemented the tuntian (military-agricultural colonies) system to restore Hebei's economy.

v.t: Roman Empire under Severan dynasty suffers trade recession and inflation
v.s: To reward the army, Severus greatly increased military pay, causing the silver content of the denarius to fall from 50% to 40%, and prices soared. Meanwhile, war with Parthia disrupted Silk Road trade, and piracy in the Mediterranean grew due to insufficient naval funding, hindering inter-provincial commerce. These economic problems foreshadowed the Crisis of the Third Century.

v.t: Ardashir I of the Sasanian Empire establishes centralized administrative system
v.s: Ardashir I, founder of the Sasanian dynasty (formally established in 224), had begun consolidating his power in Pars (southwestern Iran) around 204. He restored Zoroastrianism as state religion and created the offices of 'chancellor', 'commander of the army', and 'high priest', establishing a bureaucracy loyal to the monarch rather than local nobles, laying the administrative foundation for future Sasanian expansion.

v.t: Romanization of Gaul continues; Christianity spreads in southern Gallic cities
v.s: After two centuries of Roman rule, the aristocracy of Gaul (modern France) had fully adopted Latin language, Roman law, and lifestyle. Cities like Lugdunum, Narbo, and Arelate were filled with Roman theaters, baths, and aqueducts. Christianity spread from Syria via merchants and formed congregations in Gaul; Irenaeus, as bishop of Lugdunum, wrote against Gnosticism.

v.t: Cai Lun's improved papermaking technology spreads in the Yellow River region of Eastern Han
v.s: Eastern Han eunuch Cai Lun made plant-fiber paper from tree bark, hemp, rags, and fishing nets in 105 AD, known as 'Marquis Cai's paper.' By around 205, this papermaking technique gradually spread from Luoyang to culturally advanced regions like Jizhou and Qingzhou, replacing expensive bamboo slips and silk, greatly reducing the cost of knowledge dissemination and promoting culture and education.

v.t: Syrian teenager Elagabalus proclaimed Roman emperor by his grandmother
v.s: Emperor Macrinus lost popular support due to fiscal austerity. Julia Maesa, widow of the Severan dynasty, proclaimed her 14‑year‑old grandson Elagabalus as emperor in Syria, claiming he was Caracalla's son. Eastern legions defected; Macrinus was defeated at the Battle of Antioch, fled, and was executed in Cappadocia. Elagabalus became emperor and imposed the worship of the Syrian sun god on Rome.

v.t: Cao Cao attacks and destroys Yuan Tan, pacifying the whole of Jizhou
v.s: After seizing Yecheng, Yuan Tan regrouped in Qingzhou and united with Yuan Shang to counterattack Cao Cao. Cao Cao fought a fierce battle at Nanpi (modern Nanpi, Hebei), where Yuan Tan was killed. Cao Cao then defeated Gao Gan in Bingzhou and Yuan Xi and Yuan Shang in Youzhou, essentially controlling all territory north of the Yellow River and becoming the most powerful warlord of the late Han.

v.t: Cao Cao issues the 'Edict on Grain Collection', implementing a fixed land tax
v.s: After pacifying Hebei, Cao Cao addressed the chaotic tax system of the late Eastern Han by issuing the 'Edict on Grain Collection,' which stipulated that each mu of land should pay four sheng of millet, and each household pay two bolts of silk and two jin of floss silk, forbidding powerful families from imposing extra levies. This policy lightened the burden on self‑farming peasants and increased government revenue, forming a key institutional foundation for the economy of the Cao Wei regime.

v.t: Cao Cao attacks and destroys Gao Gan, governor of Bingzhou, securing full control north of the Yellow River
v.s: Gao Gan, Yuan Shao's nephew, held Bingzhou (modern Shanxi). After destroying Yuan Tan, Cao Cao marched north to attack Huguan (modern Changzhi, Shanxi). Gao Gan sought help from the Xiongnu but failed; he fled to Jingzhou to take refuge with Liu Biao, but was killed on the way. Cao Cao completely pacified Bingzhou, thereby unifying all of northern China. The southern warlords Liu Biao, Sun Quan, and Liu Bei could no longer oppose Cao Cao alone.

v.t: Roman emperor Severus expands palace on Palatine Hill, builds Arch of Severus
v.s: To commemorate his victory over Parthia, Septimius Severus built the triple‑triumphal Arch of Severus at the northwestern end of the Roman Forum, with reliefs depicting the capture of the Parthian capital Ctesiphon. He also expanded the imperial residence on the Palatine Hill, connecting several Republican‑era aristocratic houses into an early palace complex. Thereafter, the Roman emperor's residence was known as the 'Palatium'.

v.t: Cao Cao climbs Mount Jieshi and composes 'View of the Blue Sea' while on campaign against the Wuhuan
v.s: To eradicate the last of Yuan Shao's forces, Cao Cao led an expedition against the Wuhuan in 207 (in present‑day western Liaoning). While passing the coast near Changli, he climbed Mount Jieshi (near modern Qinhuangdao, Hebei) and, facing the sea, wrote the famous line 'Eastward I climb Mount Jieshi to view the blue sea.' This became the first complete landscape poem in Chinese literary history. Written in four‑character metre, it displays the author's ambition to unify the empire.

v.t: Cao Cao crushes the Wuhuan cavalry at White Wolf Mountain, unifying northern China
v.s: Following the advice of his strategist Guo Jia, Cao Cao marched light and fast. He exited through the Lulong Pass (modern Qianxi, Hebei) and met the main Wuhuan cavalry at White Wolf Mountain (modern Kazuo, Liaoning). With Zhang Liao as the vanguard, he launched a sudden attack before the enemy could form up. The Wuhuan leader Tadun was killed, and more than 200,000 Wuhuan surrendered. Yuan Shang and Yuan Xi fled to Liaodong to join Gongsun Kang, who, fearing Cao Cao, sent their heads to Cao Cao. Thus all of northern China was unified.

v.t: Roman Africa Province becomes the empire's breadbasket, shipping huge amounts of grain to Rome
v.s: Emperor Severus strengthened his rule over Africa Province. Large estates in modern Tunisia and Algeria expanded olive and grain cultivation using Roman hydraulic technology. Ports such as Carthage and Utica each year shipped about 8 million modii (about 6,000 tons) of wheat to Rome, along with large quantities of olive oil. Tax revenues from these agricultural products were a vital part of the imperial budget.

v.t: Zhuge Liang presents the Longzhong Plan and joins Liu Bei as his advisor
v.s: Liu Bei visited Zhuge Liang three times at his thatched cottage in Longzhong. Zhuge Liang laid out his famous Longzhong Plan: occupy Jingzhou and Yizhou, ally with Eastern Wu, and when the time was right, launch two campaigns from Jingzhou and Hanzhong to conquer the north. This strategic blueprint gave Liu Bei's faction a clear political direction. Liu Bei persuaded Zhuge Liang to leave his retreat and appointed him as Army Advisor General. Zhuge Liang became Liu Bei's most important strategist.

v.t: Liu Bei sends Zhuge Liang to Jiangdong to negotiate the Sun–Liu alliance against Cao Cao
v.s: After Cao Cao's forces threatened Jingzhou and Liu Bei fled to Xiakou, Zhuge Liang volunteered to go with Lu Su to meet Sun Quan at Chaisang. In court, Zhuge Liang convinced Sun Quan to ally with Liu Bei against Cao Cao, pointing out that Cao's army was weary, unfamiliar with naval warfare, and had an unstable rear. Sun Quan decided to ally with Liu Bei and ordered Zhou Yu and Cheng Pu to lead 30,000 elite troops to join Liu Bei's 20,000.

v.t: Teenage Roman emperor Elagabalus killed; Severus Alexander succeeds
v.s: Elagabalus was notorious for his debauchery, forcing the Senate and Roman nobility to worship the Syrian sun god and marrying a Vestal Virgin, provoking widespread disgust. His grandmother Julia Maesa, to protect her position, orchestrated the assassination of Elagabalus and his mother, then proclaimed her 13‑year‑old cousin Severus Alexander as emperor, allowing the Severan dynasty to continue.

v.t: Germanic tribes begin forming confederations due to climate change, frequently raid Roman borders
v.s: Due to climate cooling and population pressure, Germanic tribes east of the Rhine began to unite. The Alamanni confederation formed in Germania, no longer content with small‑scale raiding but assembling armies of thousands to try to breach the Roman border. The Franks also formed a loose confederation controlling the lower Rhine region.

v.t: Cao Cao seizes Jingzhou; Liu Cong surrenders
v.s: Cao Cao led a large army south to attack Jingzhou. The governor of Jingzhou, Liu Biao, happened to die of illness, and his son Liu Cong succeeded him. Urged by Kuai Yue and Fu Xun, Liu Cong surrendered all of Jingzhou to Cao Cao. Liu Bei, who had been staying in Jingzhou, retreated from Fancheng but was routed at Changban by Cao Chun's Tiger and Leopard Cavalry; only the arrival of Guan Yu's naval force saved him, allowing him to flee to Xiakou. Cao Cao occupied Jiangling, gaining a large fleet and naval forces.

v.t: Sun–Liu allied forces defeat Cao Cao at Red Cliffs using fire attack, establishing the Three Kingdoms pattern
v.s: Cao Cao led about 200,000 men downstream along the Yangtze. The Sun–Liu allied force of about 50,000, commanded by Zhou Yu, Cheng Pu, and Zhuge Liang, met them. The two sides fought at Red Cliffs (northwest of modern Chibi, Hubei). Cao's troops, unaccustomed to naval warfare, chained their ships together to reduce rolling. Zhou Yu's subordinate Huang Gai feigned surrender and then, taking advantage of a southeast wind, steered fire ships laden with kindling and oil into Cao's naval encampment. The fire spread, destroying the chained ships and the shore camps. Cao Cao burned his surviving ships and retreated through Huarong Road, losing more than half his men to mud and disease. The Battle of Red Cliffs shattered Cao Cao's plan to unify the south. Liu Bei seized the four commanderies of southern Jingzhou, and Sun Quan consolidated Jiangdong, forming the initial Three Kingdoms pattern.

v.t: Roman emperor Severus, on his deathbed, tells his sons: 'Enrich the soldiers; nothing else matters'
v.s: Roman emperor Septimius Severus died of illness at Eboracum (York) in Britain. On his deathbed, he said to his two sons Caracalla and Geta: 'Be harmonious, enrich the soldiers, and despise all other men.' This dying utterance reflected Severus's military‑dictator mindset and foreshadowed the later pernicious role of the Roman army in politics.

v.t: Zhou Yu and Cheng Pu fight Cao Ren for Jiangling; Sun Quan and Liu Bei expand their territory
v.s: After the Battle of Red Cliffs, Zhou Yu led tens of thousands of troops to besiege Jiangling (modern Jingzhou, Hubei), defended by Cao Ren. After a year of fierce fighting, Cao Ren broke out and retreated, and Zhou Yu seized Jiangling. Meanwhile, Liu Bei took advantage of the situation to capture the four commanderies of southern Jingzhou (Wuling, Changsha, Guiyang, Lingling), proclaimed himself Governor of Jingzhou, and set up his seat at Gong'an. Sun Quan agreed to lend him part of the Nan commandery to jointly oppose Cao Cao.

v.t: Sun Quan gives his younger sister in marriage to Liu Bei to solidify the Sun–Liu alliance
v.s: After Red Cliffs, to strengthen the Sun–Liu alliance, Sun Quan followed Zhou Yu's advice and gave his younger sister (called Lady Sun in folklore, later Sun Shangxiang) in marriage to Liu Bei. It was a political marriage; Lady Sun was brave and masculine, attended by a hundred maidservants all carrying swords, so much so that Liu Bei was afraid every time he entered her chambers. The marriage temporarily maintained Sun–Liu cooperation but later contributed to the dispute over Jingzhou.

v.t: Eastern Wu general Zhou Yu dies of illness at Baqiu, aged 36
v.s: Zhou Yu proposed to Sun Quan a campaign to conquer Yizhou, but on his way back to Jiangling, at Baqiu (modern Yueyang, Hunan), his old battle wounds opened and he died in camp. Zhou Yu was a master of music, magnanimous and lofty. He commanded the Sun–Liu allied forces at Red Cliffs to a great victory over Cao Cao and was a key figure in the founding of Eastern Wu. In his final letter he recommended Lu Su as his successor. Sun Quan wept: 'Gongjin had the talent of a true king‑maker; now he has died young. On whom can I rely?'

v.t: Cao Cao issues a 'Call for Talent', emphasizing 'employment based solely on ability'
v.s: At Yecheng, Cao Cao issued the 'Call for Talent,' openly proposing a principle of 'employment based solely on ability.' He declared: 'If we required that only incorruptible men be used, then how could Duke Huan of Qi have become hegemon?' He proclaimed that even men 'with stains on their reputation, laughable conduct, or even lacking benevolence and filial piety but having the skills to govern the state and command armies' should be recommended. This policy broke the Han tradition of selecting officials by moral reputation and enabled many people of low birth but real talent to enter the Cao Wei administration.

v.t: At Liu Zhang's invitation, Liu Bei leads his army into Shu and stations at Jiameng Pass
v.s: Liu Zhang, governor of Yizhou, heeding the advice of Zhang Song and Fa Zheng, invited Liu Bei to lead his army into Shu to help attack Zhang Lu of Hanzhong. Liu Bei left Zhuge Liang and Guan Yu to defend Jingzhou and took over 20,000 men, including Pang Tong, Huang Zhong, and Wei Yan, up the Yangtze. Liu Zhang himself welcomed Liu Bei at Fucheng, giving him additional troops and grain and ordering him to garrison Jiameng Pass (modern Guangyuan, Sichuan). Liu Bei did not immediately attack Zhang Lu but instead won hearts and minds in Shu.

v.t: Roman emperor Caracalla kills his brother Geta to consolidate power
v.s: After Severus died in 211, Caracalla and Geta succeeded jointly. Caracalla, wanting sole rule, killed his 22‑year‑old brother Geta and his supporters in the presence of their mother, Julia Domna. Caracalla then ordered the damnatio memoriae of Geta (destroying all inscriptions, coins, and statues bearing his name and image) and used this as a pretext to purge about 20,000 of Geta's supporters, including senators and Praetorian officers.

v.t: Cao Cao campaigns west, defeats Ma Chao and Han Sui at the Battle of Weinan
v.s: After his defeat at Red Cliffs, Cao Cao turned west to attack Ma Chao and Han Sui in Guanzhong. Ma Chao allied with Han Sui and ten other warlords from Guanzhong, fielding a total of 100,000 men and holding Tong Pass. Cao Cao employed Jia Xu's stratagem of sowing discord: he wrote a letter to Han Sui, deliberately blotching and altering it, so that Ma Chao suspected Han Sui of colluding with Cao Cao. When the alliance fell apart, Cao Cao launched a general attack at Weinan. Ma Chao was badly defeated and fled to Liangzhou, while Han Sui was beaten and retreated to Huangzhong. Guanzhong was thus pacified by Cao Cao.

v.t: Caracalla issues the Antonine Constitution, granting Roman citizenship to all free men in the empire
v.s: Roman emperor Caracalla (full name Marcus Aurelius Antoninus) issued an edict granting Roman citizenship to all free males in the empire, and free women the same rights as Roman women. The primary motive was to broaden the tax base, as citizenship carried obligations such as inheritance tax and a tax on manumission of slaves. This measure dramatically reduced the political value of Roman citizenship; provincial elites, instead of aspiring to citizenship, began to pursue military careers, accelerating the integration of Roman citizens and provincial subjects.

v.t: Sun Quan moves his capital to Moling and builds Stone City, renaming it Jianye
v.s: Following the advice of Zhang Hong, Zhuge Liang, and others, Sun Quan moved his administrative center from Jingkou (modern Zhenjiang) west to Moling (modern Nanjing). On Qingliang Mountain, using natural topography, he built Stone City. The city relied on the Yangtze for defense and housed the navy and arms depots. Thereafter Sun Quan renamed Moling as Jianye, meaning 'establishing imperial enterprise.' Jianye became the political and economic center of Eastern Wu and marked the beginning of Nanjing as the capital of six dynasties.

v.t: Cao Cao's chief strategist Xun Yu commits suicide for opposing Cao Cao's elevation to duke
v.s: Cao Cao planned to be made Duke of Wei, adding ten commanderies to his fief and establishing a bureaucracy equivalent to that of the imperial court. Xun Yu argued, 'We originally raised a righteous army to restore the court and bring peace to the state; this should not be done.' He openly expressed opposition. Cao Cao bore a grudge and, using a campaign against Sun Quan as a pretext, ordered Xun Yu to accompany the army to Qiao County (modern Bozhou, Anhui) and then left him at Shouchun. Falling ill at Shouchun, Xun Yu received an empty food box from Cao Cao, a hint to commit suicide, and took poison. He was 50. Xun Yu's death marked Cao Cao's complete break from the Han framework.

v.t: Liu Bei breaks with Liu Zhang and captures Fucheng
v.s: The conspiracy of Zhang Song to help Liu Bei was exposed. Liu Zhang executed Zhang Song and ordered all passes not to let Liu Bei through. Following Pang Tong's plan, Liu Bei, under the pretext of returning to Jingzhou, summoned Yang Huai and Gao Pei, commanders of the Baishui garrison, accused them of disrespect, executed them, and absorbed their troops. Liu Bei then marched to Fucheng (modern Mianyang, Sichuan), defeating the forces of Liu Zhang's subordinates Liu Gui and Zhang Ren. Liu Bei established a foothold in Yizhou and began besieging Luocheng.

v.t: Emperor Xian of Han appoints Cao Cao as Duke of Wei, establishes state of Wei, grants ten commanderies
v.s: Under pressure from Cao Cao, Emperor Xian of Han issued an edict appointing Cao Cao as Duke of Wei, granting him the Nine Bestowals, and establishing a Wei bureaucracy. Ten commanderies of Jizhou were given as his fief, with the capital at Yecheng. This was the first time in the Eastern Han that a man not of the imperial clan was made a duke (the only previous case was Wang Mang as Duke of Anhan). The establishment of the Duchy of Wei signified that Cao Cao had formally separated himself from the Han bureaucracy, with his own ancestral temple, imperial secretariat, and bureaucracy, taking a decisive step toward replacing the Han.

v.t: Empress Fu, wife of Emperor Xian of Han, deposed and executed after failed plot against Cao Cao
v.s: Empress Fu had earlier written to her father Fu Wan, plotting to assassinate Cao Cao, but Fu Wan dared not act. In 214, the plot was exposed. Cao Cao forced Emperor Xian to depose Empress Fu and sent the censor‑in‑chief Xi Lu to take her seal. Empress Fu was imprisoned and died, and her two sons were poisoned. Cao Cao then made his own daughter Cao Jie empress, further tightening his control over the imperial house.

v.t: Liu Bei captures Chengdu and takes full control of Yizhou
v.s: Liu Bei besieged Luocheng for a year; Pang Tong was hit by a stray arrow and killed. Liu Bei summoned Zhuge Liang, Zhang Fei, and Zhao Yun to reinforce him in Shu. After Luocheng fell, Liu Bei advanced to besiege Chengdu. At that time, Ma Chao came over from Zhang Lu and camped north of the city, spreading fear throughout Chengdu. Under the persuasion of Qiao Zhou and others, Liu Zhang opened the city gates and surrendered. Liu Bei thus took possession of Yizhou, proclaiming himself Governor of Yizhou, and employed many talented men from Shu such as Fa Zheng, Li Yan, Huang Quan, and Liu Ba. This achieved the first step of the Longzhong Plan: occupying Jing and Yi.

v.t: Roman emperor Caracalla campaigns against the Germans, builds forts along the Rhine
v.s: To gain military glory and booty to solidify his rule, Caracalla led an expedition into Germania. He repaired and built several legionary fortresses along the Rhine and fought the Alamanni in the Mainz region. In reality, he was more engaged in deterrent shows of force and buying off German chieftains rather than large‑scale conquest. His march revealed the problem of lax discipline in the army.

v.t: Cao Cao leads army into Longyou, subdues Yang Qiu, pacifies Liangzhou
v.s: Xiahou Yuan campaigned in Liangzhou against the remnants of Ma Chao and the Di and Qiang rebels. Cao Cao personally led an army into Longyou (eastern Gansu). Han Sui was killed by his subordinates, who presented his head. Xiahou Yuan defeated the Di king of Xingguo at Changli (eastern Gansu) and besieged and beheaded Song Jian at Fuhan (modern Linxia, Gansu), thereby pacifying Longyou. Cao Cao left Xiahou Yuan to guard Chang'an and returned to Yecheng. The pacification of Liangzhou extended Cao Wei's western territory to the upper reaches of the Yellow River.

v.t: Caracalla campaigns east against Parthia, plunders Mesopotamia
v.s: Using as a pretext the refusal of Parthian king Artabanus IV to give him his daughter in marriage, Caracalla led an expedition east. The Roman army marched south, capturing towns in northern Mesopotamia and reaching near the Parthian capital Ctesiphon. Instead of fighting a decisive battle, Caracalla massacred a group of Parthian nobles in a Greek temple. The Parthians hated him and prepared to retaliate.

v.t: Sun Quan sends Zhuge Jin to negotiate with Liu Bei; dispute over Jingzhou temporarily eased
v.s: After Red Cliffs, Liu Bei had borrowed the Nan commandery, but Sun Quan believed Jingzhou belonged to Eastern Wu. Sun Quan sent Zhuge Jin (Zhuge Liang's elder brother) to Chengdu to demand the return of the three commanderies of Changsha, Guiyang, and Lingling. Liu Bei refused, saying, 'Wait until I have taken Liangzhou, then I will return Jingzhou.' Sun Quan was furious and sent Lü Meng to seize the three commanderies, while Liu Bei sent Guan Yu to contest them. Then, because Cao Cao attacked Hanzhong and Liu Bei worried about the safety of Yizhou, he negotiated a truce with Sun Quan. They agreed to divide Jingzhou along the Xiang River: Changsha, Jiangxia, and Guiyang to Sun Quan; Nan, Lingling, and Wuling to Liu Bei.

v.t: Cao Cao campaigns against Zhang Lu and captures Hanzhong
v.s: Taking advantage of the conflict between Liu Bei and Sun Quan over Jingzhou, Cao Cao led 100,000 men west to attack Zhang Lu in Hanzhong. Cao's army passed through the Chencang Road, exited San Pass, and captured Yangping Pass. Zhang Lu's subordinates Yang Ang and Yang Ren defended the mountain passes but were defeated by Xiahou Yuan and Zhang He. Zhang Lu fled to Bazhong and soon surrendered. Cao Cao occupied Hanzhong. Sima Yi and Liu Ye advised him to take advantage of the victory and march south to attack Shu, but Cao Cao replied, 'Human hearts are never content; having seized Long, one then looks to Shu?' He withdrew, leaving Xiahou Yuan and Zhang He to guard Hanzhong.

v.t: Caracalla invades the heart of Parthia, pillaging and burning
v.s: After devastating Mesopotamia, Caracalla continued his invasion into Parthian heartland. His army plundered cities such as Edessa and Carrhae and even dug up local royal tombs for treasure. Parthian king Artabanus IV assembled a large army to meet him, but due to internal dissension among some nobles, he could not immediately counterattack. Caracalla's actions enraged all of Parthia and set the stage for his assassination the next year.

v.t: Cao Cao executes the famous scholar Cui Yan, suppressing the gentry
v.s: Cui Yan was a native of Qinghe Dongwucheng, a former subordinate of Yuan Shao who later submitted to Cao Cao. He was upright and outspoken. When Cao Cao was about to become King of Wei, Cui Yan opposed him and criticized the Nine Bestowals. Someone denounced Cui Yan for irreverence in his correspondence, and Cao Cao ordered him to commit suicide under the charge of 'arrogance and slander.' Cui Yan's death was an example of Cao Cao striking at the powerful gentry families and suppressing dissent, reflecting Cao Cao's suspicious nature in his later years.

v.t: Emperor Xian of Han appoints Cao Cao as King of Wei, ranking above all feudal kings
v.s: Under pressure, Emperor Xian of Han appointed Cao Cao as King of Wei, his status higher than all other feudal kings, with imperial regalia (such as a twelve‑pendant crown, gold root‑carriage chariot, six‑horse team, and five‑colored secondary chariots). Cao Cao established his royal capital at Yecheng, set up an ancestral temple, and named Cao Pi as crown prince of Wei. Although he still nominally called himself a Han minister, Cao Cao was de facto emperor, and the Eastern Han existed in name only.

v.t: Roman emperor Severus Alexander killed by soldiers, escalating the Crisis of the Third Century
v.s: Roman emperor Severus Alexander fought the Germans at Mainz. When he tried to solve the problem with money rather than force, he provoked the soldiers' discontent. The Praetorian Guard mutinied, rushed into the emperor's tent, and killed Alexander (aged 27) and his mother. The soldiers then proclaimed Maximinus Thrax, a low‑born officer from Thrace, as emperor. This event ushered in the chaotic military anarchy phase of the Roman 'Crisis of the Third Century'.

v.t: Roman emperor Caracalla assassinated near Carrhae
v.s: During his eastern campaign against Parthia, Caracalla was assassinated near Carrhae (modern Harran, Turkey) while visiting a temple. The assassin was a Praetorian officer named Julius Martialis. The assassin may have been instigated by the Parthians or the Praetorian prefect Macrinus. Caracalla was 29. His death ended the direct rule of the Severan dynasty.

v.t: Cao Cao and Liu Bei fight fiercely in Hanzhong; both sides reach a stalemate
v.s: After Liu Bei was secure in Yizhou, Fa Zheng advised him to seize Hanzhong. Liu Bei personally led his army to attack Hanzhong, first sending Zhang Fei, Ma Chao, and Wu Lan to attack Xiabian (modern Cheng County, Gansu), but they were defeated by Cao Hong. Liu Bei's main force stationed at Yangping Pass, facing Xiahou Yuan and Zhang He. Cao Cao led a large army to reinforce, and the two sides were locked in stalemate for months, each winning and losing some encounters. Due to the difficulty of transporting grain, Cao Cao finally withdrew in 219, leaving Cao Hong and Cao Zhen to defend the area.

v.t: Great plague ravages Central China; several of the 'Seven Scholars of Jian'an' die
v.s: From the winter of 217 to spring 218, a severe plague swept northern China, causing many deaths in Yecheng, Xuchang, Luoyang, and elsewhere. Cao Pi lamented in a letter to Wu Zhi: 'Last year a pestilence struck, taking many of our old friends. Xu, Chen, Ying, and Liu all died at once.' Of the Seven Scholars of Jian'an, Xu Gan, Chen Lin, Ying Yang, and Liu Zhen all died of plague in the same year; Wang Can had died the previous year. The plague dealt a heavy blow to the scholar‑official class and affected Cao Wei's cultural development.

v.t: Maximinus Thrax proclaimed Roman emperor by the army
v.s: After Severus Alexander was killed, the Praetorian Guard proclaimed Maximinus as emperor. Maximinus was huge in stature and immensely strong; he had demonstrated his prowess before Alexander. He was the first emperor of barbarian (Thracian) origin who had never set foot in Rome. Immediately after his accession, he tripled military pay, leading to extreme financial strain that could only be relieved by confiscating aristocratic property and robbing temples.

v.t: Syrian teenager Elagabalus proclaimed emperor in opposition to Macrinus
v.s: Julia Maesa, mother of the late Caracalla, fomented a rebellion in Syria and proclaimed her 14‑year‑old grandson Elagabalus as emperor, claiming he was Caracalla's illegitimate son. Macrinus sent an army to suppress the uprising, but the two sides met in battle near Antioch. When the battle turned against him, Macrinus fled but was caught and killed. The Senate, under pressure, recognized Elagabalus as emperor. The boy‑emperor was fanatically devoted to Syrian sun‑worship and forced the Romans to revere Syrian deities.

v.t: Liu Bei and Cao Cao fight fiercely at Mount Dingjun and the Han River; the contest for Hanzhong intensifies
v.s: Liu Bei, with Fa Zheng and Huang Zhong, confronted Xiahou Yuan's Cao forces at Mount Dingjun (modern Mian County, Shaanxi). Fa Zheng devised a plan: Liu Bei set fire to the Cao army's palisades at night; Xiahou Yuan came to put out the fire, and Huang Zhong, from a high position, launched a surprise attack, killing Xiahou Yuan in the melee. The Cao army then promoted Zhang He as commander, and Cao Cao himself led a large force to reinforce. Liu Bei held the strategic points and refused to give battle, while sending Zhao Yun and Huang Zhong to raid Cao Cao's grain supply lines. With his grain nearly exhausted, Cao Cao withdrew in 219, and Liu Bei occupied all of Hanzhong.

v.t: Guan Yu launches the Fancheng campaign, floods seven armies, terrifies the Central Plains
v.s: After Liu Bei occupied Hanzhong, Guan Yu set out from Jiangling north to attack Fancheng (north of modern Xiangyang, Hubei), which was defended by Cao Ren. Cao Cao sent Yu Jin and Pang De with about 30,000 men to relieve the siege. In August, the Han River flooded. Guan Yu used large warships to attack the Cao camps, flooded the seven armies, captured Yu Jin, and executed Pang De. Guan Yu then besieged Xiangyang, and Cao Cao even considered moving the capital to avoid him. Guan Yu's reputation spread far and wide, and many counties in the area responded to him. This was the peak of Guan Yu's military career.

v.t: Liu Bei proclaims himself King of Hanzhong at Mianyang and establishes the Hanzhong King's office
v.s: Urged by his ministers, Liu Bei erected an altar at Mianyang (modern Mian County, Shaanxi) and declared himself King of Hanzhong, claiming to be continuing the Han imperial line as a member of the Han clan. He made Xu Jing Grand Tutor, Fa Zheng Minister of the Interior, Guan Yu General of the Front, Zhang Fei General of the Right, Ma Chao General of the Left, and Huang Zhong General of the Rear. Liu Bei's authority in Shu was further consolidated, and his military and economic strength matched that of Cao Wei and Eastern Wu in a tripartite balance.

v.t: Lü Meng captures Jiangling in a 'white‑sailed boat' ruse; Guan Yu flees to Maicheng and is killed
v.s: Eastern Wu general Lü Meng took advantage of Guan Yu's northern campaign and the weakness in his rear. Disguising merchant ships, he hid his elite soldiers below deck and sailed west day and night, capturing Jiangling and Gong'an by surprise. Mi Fang and Shi Ren surrendered. When Guan Yu heard the news, he turned back. He repeatedly sent envoys to Lü Meng, who treated the families of Guan Yu's soldiers well, destroying their morale. Guan Yu's men gradually deserted. He retreated to Maicheng. Sun Quan tried to persuade him to surrender but failed, then sent Zhu Ran and Pan Zhang to cut off his escape. Guan Yu and his son Guan Ping broke out and reached Linju (modern Yuan'an, Hubei), where they were captured by Ma Zhong, a subordinate of Pan Zhang, and executed. Sun Quan sent Guan Yu's head to Cao Cao at Luoyang; Cao Cao buried the head with noble rites, while Sun Quan buried Guan Yu's body at Dangyang.

v.t: Roman emperor Elagabalus forces the Senate to accept the Syrian sun god, causing cultural conflict
v.s: Elagabalus brought the sacred black stone of the sun god from Emesa in Syria to Rome, built a temple on the Palatine, and ordered the Senate and knights to perform rituals. He married a Vestal Virgin and appointed Syrian priests to high Roman offices. Conservative Romans were extremely resentful, and the army and Senate increasingly despised him. This cultural conflict accelerated Elagabalus's downfall.

v.t: Teotihuacan in Mesoamerica enters its flourishing period; Sun Pyramid expanded
v.s: Teotihuacan, located 50 km northeast of modern Mexico City, was at its height from the 1st to 7th centuries AD. Around 220 AD, the city's population exceeded 100,000, covering 20 square kilometers, making it the largest city in Mesoamerica. The Sun Pyramid was expanded for the third time, reaching a height of 65 meters; the Moon Pyramid and the Temple of the Feathered Serpent were also built. Teotihuacan controlled all of Mesoamerica through obsidian trade, and its art and architecture influenced later Maya and Aztec civilizations.

v.t: Goths launch first large‑scale invasion of the Roman Empire across the Danube
v.s: The Goths, a Scythian‑descended people, formed a tribal confederation in the Dniester region. Around 220 AD, the Goths crossed the Danube and raided the Roman provinces of Dacia and Moesia. The Roman army, under‑strength, could only repel them but not root them out. Thereafter, the Goths raided the Balkans every year, becoming one of the greatest threats to the Roman Empire's northern border.

v.t: Cao Pi forces Emperor Xian of Han to abdicate, establishes the Cao Wei dynasty; Eastern Han ends
v.s: Cao Cao died of illness in Luoyang in January 220, and his son Cao Pi succeeded as King of Wei. In October, Cao Pi ordered Emperor Xian to perform the abdication ceremony, declaring that the Han's mandate had ended. Emperor Xian offered the throne three times; Cao Pi thrice declined, then accepted, changing the era name to Huangchu, naming his dynasty Wei, and establishing the capital at Luoyang. Cao Pi granted Liu Xie the title of Duke of Shanyang, with a fief of 10,000 households, ranking above the feudal kings. The Eastern Han, which had been founded by Emperor Guangwu in 25 AD, lasted 196 years and had twelve emperors. The Three Kingdoms period formally began.

v.t: Cao Pi adopts Chen Qun's proposal and formally institutes the nine‑rank system for official recruitment
v.s: To win the support of the gentry, Emperor Wen of Wei (Cao Pi) accepted the proposal of the minister Chen Qun and established the nine‑rank system (jiupin zhongzheng). Central government officials served as zhongzheng in the provinces and commanderies, responsible for evaluating local men according to three criteria: pedigree, virtue, and talent, dividing them into nine grades as the basis for appointment. In its early stages, the system selected many talented men for Cao Wei, but later the zhongzheng were gradually monopolized by great clans, and pedigree became the only criterion, leading to the situation where 'there were no humble families in the high ranks and no powerful families in the low ranks.'

v.t: Cao Cao dies of illness in Luoyang, leaving instructions for a simple burial
v.s: Cao Cao fell seriously ill at Luoyang and, before dying, issued a 'Final Command' requesting to be buried on a hill west of Yecheng, in ordinary clothes, with no mausoleum, no mound, no trees, and no precious objects in the tomb. Cao Cao also gave detailed instructions for the care of his concubines, telling them to sell perfume and make shoes to support themselves. He was 66, and was given the posthumous title of King Wu. His death marked the end of an era, and his son Cao Pi quickly completed the transition from Han to Wei.

v.t: Cao Pi usurps the Han throne and establishes the Wei dynasty; Eastern Han ends, Three Kingdoms tripartite equilibrium forms
v.s: Cao Cao's son Cao Pi, using political maneuvers, forced Emperor Xian of Han to abdicate and ascended the throne at Luoyang, proclaiming his dynasty 'Wei' and changing the era name to Huangchu. The Eastern Han, which had been restored by Emperor Guangwu, came to an end after 195 years. Cao Pi granted Liu Xie the title of Duke of Shanyang, with a fief of 10,000 households and generous treatment. The following year, Liu Bei proclaimed himself emperor at Chengdu, naming his dynasty 'Han' (known to history as Shu Han), claiming to continue the Han imperial line. In 221, Sun Quan accepted Wei's enfeoffment as King of Wu, and in 229 he officially declared himself emperor. The Three Kingdoms tripartite equilibrium was fully established.

v.t: To avenge Guan Yu, Liu Bei mobilizes 70,000 troops to attack Wu
v.s: After proclaiming himself emperor in Chengdu, Liu Bei, ignoring the remonstrances of Zhao Yun, Zhuge Liang, and others, personally led a campaign against Eastern Wu 'to avenge Guan Yu.' Liu Bei commanded 70,000 elite Shu troops, including generals Zhang Fei (who was murdered by his own subordinates just before the campaign set out), Wu Ban, Feng Xi, and Huang Quan, and advanced east along the Yangtze, capturing Wu County and Zigui. Sun Quan sent Zhuge Jin to sue for peace, but Liu Bei refused. Sun Quan was forced to declare himself a vassal of Cao Pi and appointed Lu Xun as Grand Commander to meet the invasion with 50,000 troops.

v.t: Liu Bei ascends the imperial throne at Chengdu; his dynasty is called Han, known to history as Shu Han
v.s: Urged by his ministers including Zhuge Liang, Liu Bei set up an altar at Mount Wu in Chengdu and proclaimed himself emperor, claiming to continue the legitimate Han line. He announced that he had received an abdication edict from Emperor Xian of Han (in fact a forgery), and changed the era name to Zhangwu. He appointed Zhuge Liang as Chancellor, Xu Jing as Minister of Education, Zhang Fei as General of Agile Cavalry, and Ma Chao as General of the Flying Cavalry. The Shu Han regime was formally established, and the tripartite balance of Wei, Shu, and Wu took shape.

v.t: Roman emperor Elagabalus assassinated by the Praetorian Guard; his cousin Severus Alexander succeeds
v.s: Elagabalus's debauchery and religious fanaticism aroused the fury of the Praetorian Guard and the Senate. His grandmother Julia Maesa planned a coup, bribing the Praetorians. Elagabalus and his mother were killed in a camp on the outskirts of Rome, and their bodies were dragged through the city and thrown into the Tiber. The Senate declared the dead man a 'public enemy' and proclaimed his 13‑year‑old cousin Severus Alexander as emperor.

v.t: Emperor Wen of Wei (Cao Pi) enfeoffs Sun Quan as King of Wu with the Nine Bestowals and appoints him Governor of Jingzhou
v.s: Liu Bei's invasion of Wu forced Sun Quan to avoid a two‑front war. He declared himself a vassal of Cao Pi and accepted the title of King of Wu from Wei, together with the Nine Bestowals and the governorship of Jingzhou. Sun Quan sent Lu Xun and Zhuge Jin to maintain good relations with Wei while secretly building up his strength. Although Cao Pi had hoped to take advantage of the situation to destroy Wu, he hesitated after being advised by Liu Ye, and did not act.

v.t: Roman teenage emperor Severus Alexander ascends; Senate regains some authority
v.s: Severus Alexander was recognized as Roman emperor by the Senate. He was only 13, and real power was held by his grandmother Julia Maesa and his mother Julia Mamaea. Alexander had received a good education, respected law and the Senate, and promoted a number of talented jurists and administrators. In the early part of his reign, the Roman situation was relatively stable, but the problem of excessive military power remained.

v.t: Liu Bei suffers a crushing defeat at the Battle of Xiaoting; flees to Baidi City, dies the following year
v.s: Liu Bei's invasion force, with camps stretching 700 li from Wuxia to Xiaoting (southeast of modern Yichang, Hubei), was confronted by Lu Xun. Lu Xun adopted a strategy of holding fast and refusing battle. When the Shu army's morale had drained and the weather turned hot, Lu Xun ordered a fire attack. The Wu forces set fire downwind, burning over forty Shu camps. The Shu army was utterly routed, with tens of thousands killed or wounded, and almost all their boats and equipment were destroyed. Liu Bei fled to Baidi City (modern Fengjie, Chongqing) and renamed it Yong'an. In April of the following year, seriously ill, he summoned Zhuge Liang and entrusted him with his son, saying, 'If my son is worthy, support him; if he is not, you may take the throne yourself.' Liu Bei died, and Liu Shan succeeded him.

v.t: Liu Shan succeeds in Chengdu; Zhuge Liang opens his office and restores the alliance with Wu
v.s: After Liu Bei's death, his son Liu Shan ascended the throne, changing the era name to Jianxing. Zhuge Liang, as Chancellor and Minister over the Masses, opened his office and effectively held all power. He sent Deng Zhi as envoy to Wu, re‑establishing the Shu–Wu alliance. Seeing that Zhuge Liang was in charge, Sun Quan reciprocated by sending Zhang Wen to Shu, and the two sides agreed to attack Wei together. The Shu–Wu alliance was restored, laying the foundation for later Northern Expeditions and Wei–Wu wars.

v.t: Sun Quan accepts Wei titles but refuses to send a hostage; proclaims himself King of Wu and adopts reign name Huangwu
v.s: After his defeat at Xiaoting, Sun Quan, while nominally submissive to Wei, refused to send his son Sun Deng as a hostage to Luoyang. Cao Pi demanded the hostage several times, but was refused, and Wei–Wu relations deteriorated. Sun Quan changed his reign name to Huangwu (indicating that he was no longer using Wei's era name), established his own bureaucracy, and in effect became an independent state.

v.t: Emperor Wen of Wei (Cao Pi) launches a three‑pronged invasion of Wu, but fails to achieve his aim
v.s: Angry that Sun Quan had not sent a hostage, Cao Pi ordered a three‑pronged attack on Wu: Cao Ren attacked Ru'xu; Cao Xiu, Cao Zhen, and Zhang Liao attacked the river mouth; and Xiahou Shang and Xu Huang attacked Jiangling. The Wu army relied on the natural defenses of the Yangtze and stout walls to resist tenaciously. Zhu Huan badly defeated Cao Ren at Ruxu, and Zhu Ran held Jiangling for six months. The Wei navy had no advantage, and an epidemic spread. Cao Pi ordered a withdrawal. The Wei forces suffered heavy losses and did not achieve their goal of destroying Wu.

v.t: Roman Empire's Danube frontier partially collapses due to Gothic invasion
v.s: The Goths, together with the Carpi and other tribes, crossed the Danube in force and invaded the province of Moesia (modern Bulgaria). The Roman border forces were insufficient, and several forts fell. Emperor Severus Alexander was forced to lead a campaign in person, the first time in thirty years that a Roman emperor had marched north to fight barbarians. The Roman army fought a hard defensive battle along the Danube.

v.t: Zhuge Liang sends Deng Zhi as envoy to Wu to repair bilateral relations
v.s: After Liu Bei's death, relations between Wu and Shu Han were strained. Zhuge Liang, assessing the situation, sent the minister Deng Zhi to Jianye to reaffirm the Shu–Wu alliance. Deng Zhi pointed out to Sun Quan that Shu and Wu were as interdependent as lips and teeth, and that only by uniting could they oppose Wei. Sun Quan was convinced, broke off his vassal relationship with Wei, and re‑established the alliance with Shu. The two sides agreed to divide the Wei empire equally after its destruction. This diplomatic success created a stable rear area for Zhuge Liang's northern expeditions.

v.t: Cao Pi again personally leads an expedition against Wu, reaching Guangling but returning without success
v.s: Not resigned to the failure of his previous campaign, Cao Pi this time led the navy from Qiao County into the Wo River, then via the Huai River to Guangling (modern Yangzhou, Jiangsu). Eastern Wu had set up tight defenses along the river, with wooden stakes and chains across the water. Gazing at the turbulent Yangtze, Cao Pi sighed, 'There are capable men on the other side; it cannot be taken.' At that time, the river was freezing, and Wei ships could not move. Cao Pi had to order a retreat. The Guangling campaign began with a great show of force but ended without a single engagement.

v.t: Ardashir I of Sasanian Persia overthrows the Parthian Empire and founds the Sasanian dynasty
v.s: Ardashir I was a local noble of Pars province in Persia. He rose in rebellion against the ruling Parthian Arsacid Empire. After years of fighting, in 224 he defeated the Parthian king Artabanus IV on the plain of Hormuz, killed him, captured the capital Ctesiphon, and founded the Sasanian dynasty. Ardashir claimed to be a descendant of the Achaemenid dynasty, restored Persian legitimacy, and made Zoroastrianism the state religion. This event ended nearly 500 years of Parthian rule and reunited Persia.

v.t: Zhuge Liang vigorously develops production and prepares the military in Chengdu
v.s: As Chancellor, Zhuge Liang directed Shu Han's government. At home, he pursued a policy of 'devoting efforts to agriculture, closing the borders and giving the people rest.' He repaired the Dujiangyan irrigation system, setting up a dedicated weir administration; implemented military agricultural colonies in Hanzhong to grow grain; and established a brocade office to manage silk production, which became Shu Han's main export. At the same time, he presided over military manufacturing, improving the repeating crossbow and constructing 'wooden oxen' and 'flowing horses' for transport, building up strength for future northern expeditions.

v.t: Cao Pi's last campaign against Wu: reviews troops on the Yangtze at Guangling and returns; dies soon after
v.s: Cao Pi again mobilized more than 100,000 troops and went again to Guangling. He boarded a ship to inspect the navy, flags stretching for hundreds of li. But it was winter, the river was shallow, and the Wu defense was tight; he could not cross. Cao Pi sighed, 'Though Wei has thousands of horsemen, they are of no use here.' He ordered a retreat. He died the following year. This was the last of Cao Pi's three campaigns against Wu; he never got across the Yangtze.

v.t: Ardashir I of Sasanian Persia formally crowned 'King of Kings' at Ctesiphon
v.s: After capturing the Parthian capital Ctesiphon, Ardashir I held a grand coronation there, adopting the ancient Persian title 'King of Kings' (Shahanshah) and minting coins bearing his image, beginning the Sasanian era. He quickly campaigned east and west, reconquering Khorasan, Sistan, and other regions, extending his power to the Amu Darya in Central Asia. Sasanian Persia became a powerful eastern rival of the Roman Empire.

v.t: Zhuge Liang's southern campaign: captures Meng Huo seven times, pacifies the Nanzhong rebellion
v.s: Rebellions broke out in Shu Han's Nanzhong region (modern Yunnan, Guizhou, southern Sichuan) led by the local strongmen Yong Kai, Zhu Bao, Gao Ding, and Meng Huo. Zhuge Liang personally led a three‑pronged southern campaign, adopting a strategy of 'winning hearts first.' Around Lake Dian, Zhuge Liang defeated Meng Huo and captured and released him seven times, until Meng Huo said sincerely, 'The southerners will never rebel again.' In Nanzhong, Zhuge Liang replaced the old ruling families with locally appointed officials, and used the gold, silver, cattle, horses, and war elephants from the south to support his northern expeditions, strengthening Shu Han.

v.t: Emperor Wen of Wei (Cao Pi) dies; Sima Yi appointed as regent, beginning his rise
v.s: Cao Pi died of illness in Luoyang at the age of 40. On his deathbed, he named Cao Zhen, Chen Qun, Sima Yi, and Cao Xiu as regents to assist the crown prince Cao Rui. Although Sima Yi had held various posts, he had never been at the core of decision‑making. Being included as a regent marked his entry into the center of Cao Wei power, laying the groundwork for his eventual control of the state.

v.t: Roman emperor Severus Alexander personally campaigns against Persia; Gordian III appointed Caesar
v.s: The Sasanian Persians invaded Mesopotamia. Severus Alexander led a large army east, establishing his headquarters at Antioch in Syria, where he faced a long‑term standoff with the Persians. He promoted the young Gordian (later Gordian III) to the Senate and appointed him Caesar. However, the enormous military expenditure forced Alexander to impose taxes on the Senate, causing tension in their relationship.

v.t: Ardashir I of Sasanian Persia unifies all Persia and establishes centralized rule
v.s: Ardashir I successively conquered Media, Armenia, Azerbaijan and other regions, completing the conquest of all territories of the former Parthian Empire. He implemented administrative reforms, dividing the empire into provinces governed by royal appointees, weakening local aristocratic power. He re‑compiled Zoroastrian scriptures and established the system of sacred fire temples, strengthening religious authority. Thus the Sasanian dynasty became a great empire spanning West Asia.

v.t: Emperor Wen of Wei (Cao Pi) dies in Luoyang; Crown Prince Cao Rui succeeds
v.s: Cao Pi died at the Jiafu Palace in Luoyang at the age of 40. He was the author of the 'Canon of Essays', the first work of literary theory in Chinese literary history. He advocated stylistic reform and was an important member of the Jian'an literary circle. After his death, he received the posthumous title Emperor Wen and was buried at the Shouyang Mausoleum. Crown Prince Cao Rui (Emperor Ming of Wei) ascended the throne, changing the era name to Taihe.

v.t: Meng Da, governor of Xincheng, rebels against Wei and defects to Shu; Sima Yi marches a thousand li and captures him
v.s: Meng Da was a former Shu Han general who had surrendered to Wei, stationed at Xincheng (modern Fang County, Hubei). On the eve of Zhuge Liang's northern expedition, Zhuge Liang wrote to seduce Meng Da into defecting. Meng Da fell out with Wei Xing governor Shen Yi, and planned a rebellion. Sima Yi, on receiving the emergency report, without waiting for an imperial edict, marched day and night, covering 1,200 li in eight days, and surrounded Xincheng before Meng Da could react. After sixteen days the city fell and Meng Da was executed. Sima Yi's rapid action foiled Zhuge Liang's plan to attack from both north and south.

v.t: Border clashes erupt between the Sasanian Empire and the Roman Empire in Mesopotamia
v.s: Sasanian Persian king Ardashir I continued to expand westward, invading northern Mesopotamia, which was under Roman control. Roman emperor Severus Alexander led his army to meet the threat, and the two sides fought near Nisibis. The Persian cavalry was highly mobile, but the Roman legions were solidly defended. The fighting reached a stalemate, and a peace treaty was concluded the following year, maintaining the existing borders.

v.t: Zhuge Liang presents the 'Chu Shi Biao', leads his army to Hanzhong to prepare for the northern expedition
v.s: After pacifying the south, Zhuge Liang felt the time was ripe for a northern expedition. He presented the 'Chu Shi Biao' to Liu Shan, expressing his determination to 'conquer the north, dedicate my entire might, drive out the traitors, and restore the Han.' He then led his army to Hanzhong, encamped at Mianyang (modern Mian County, Shaanxi), drilled his troops, repaired equipment, and waited for the right moment to launch the expedition. This marked the beginning of Zhuge Liang's northern expeditions.

v.t: Zhuge Liang's first northern expedition: Ma Su loses Jieting, leading to complete failure
v.s: Zhuge Liang feinted an attack via the Xie Valley route but actually led his main force out of Mount Qi (modern Li County, Gansu). Nan'an, Tianshui, and Anding commanderies rebelled and responded to him, throwing the Guanzhong region into turmoil. Emperor Ming of Wei personally traveled to Chang'an to take command and sent Zhang He to the rescue. Zhuge Liang ordered Ma Su to hold Jieting (modern Zhuanglang, Gansu). Ma Su disobeyed orders by camping on a hill; Zhang He cut off his water supply, and the Shu army broke up in disorder. Zhuge Liang, with tears, executed Ma Su and withdrew all his forces to Hanzhong. The first northern expedition failed.

v.t: Zhuge Liang's second northern expedition: besieges Chencang but fails to take it
v.s: In the winter of the same year, taking advantage of the fact that the main Wei forces were on the eastern front, Zhuge Liang led tens of thousands of troops out of San Pass and besieged Chencang (modern Baoji, Shaanxi). The Wei commander Hao Zhao had only a thousand men, but the city walls were strong. Zhuge Liang used various siege engines such as siege towers, battering rams, and tunnels; Hao Zhao defended with flaming arrows, firebombs, boiling oil, and other countermeasures. After more than twenty days of fierce fighting, with Wei reinforcements about to arrive and Zhuge Liang's supplies exhausted, he withdrew. The Wei army pursued, but Zhuge Liang ambushed and defeated them.

v.t: Eastern Wu's Lu Xun destroys Cao Xiu at Shiting, shocking the entire Wei state
v.s: Cao Xiu, Grand Marshal of Wei, led 100,000 infantry and cavalry south to fight a decisive battle with Eastern Wu at Shiting (modern Qianshan, Anhui). Eastern Wu's Grand Commander Lu Xun, together with Zhu Huan and Quan Cong, attacked in three columns. Lu Xun attacked Cao Xiu from the front while Zhu Huan and Quan Cong enveloped his flanks. Cao Xiu was routed, losing over 10,000 dead and all his supplies. Mortified, Cao Xiu fell ill and died soon after. This battle left Wei unable to mount a large‑scale attack on Wu for some time.

v.t: Sun Quan formally proclaims himself emperor at Wuchang, with the dynasty name Wu, era name Huanglong
v.s: Urged by his ministers, Sun Quan formally ascended the imperial throne at Wuchang (modern Ezhou, Hubei), proclaiming the dynasty name Wu, and changing the era name to Huanglong. He posthumously honored his father Sun Jian as Emperor Wulie of Wu, and his elder brother Sun Ce as Prince Huan of Changsha. He appointed Lu Xun as Senior General‑in‑Chief and Zhuge Jin as General‑in‑Chief. After Sun Quan proclaimed himself emperor, Shu Han sent Chen Zhen to congratulate him. The two sides agreed that after destroying Wei they would divide the empire equally: the territory west of Hangu Pass to Shu, and east of the pass to Wu.

v.t: After proclaiming himself emperor, Sun Quan formally moves the capital to Jianye
v.s: To facilitate rule over the lower Yangtze region, Sun Quan moved the capital from Wuchang back to Jianye (modern Nanjing). He built the Taichu Palace, 300 zhang in circumference, with five gates on the south side. After the move, Jianye became the political and economic center of the middle and lower Yangtze, with a rapidly growing population. Lu Xun remained at Wuchang to oversee defense of the upper reaches.

v.t: Roman jurists compile the 'Codex Gregorianus', systematically organizing imperial edicts
v.s: The Roman jurist Gregorius was commissioned to compile a codex systematically organizing the imperial constitutions (edicts) from the reign of Hadrian onward. The Codex Gregorianus, though lost, initiated the Roman tradition of official codification and later influenced the Theodosian Code and Justinian's Code. Roman jurisprudence remained at a high level during this period.

v.t: Zhuge Liang's third northern expedition: takes Wudu and Yinping commanderies
v.s: Zhuge Liang sent Chen Shi to attack the Wei commanderies of Wudu (modern Longnan, Gansu) and Yinping (modern Wen County, Gansu). The Wei Inspector of Yongzhou, Guo Huai, led troops to rescue them, but Zhuge Liang personally led his army to Jianwei (modern Xihe, Gansu) to block him. Guo Huai retreated, and the Shu forces smoothly took the two commanderies. This was the only northern expedition in which Zhuge Liang fully achieved his strategic objective; the acquisition of these two commanderies increased Shu Han's population and strategic depth.

v.t: Shu Han and Eastern Wu conclude a formal alliance, agreeing to jointly attack Wei
v.s: After Sun Quan proclaimed himself emperor, Zhuge Liang sent Chen Zhen to Wu to congratulate him and renew the alliance. The treaty stipulated that 'Han and Wu are one body and will together attack Cao Wei', and that 'if Wei suffers internal disorder, the two sides will attack simultaneously.' They agreed that after Wei was destroyed, Yu, Qing, Xu, and You provinces would go to Wu, while Yan, Ji, Bing, and Liang provinces would go to Shu; the Si province (the capital region) would be divided at Hangu Pass. This paper division of territory was never realized, but it strengthened the Shu–Wu alliance.

v.t: Sun Quan sends generals Wei Wen and Zhuge Zhi with a fleet of 10,000 men to seek the legendary island of Yizhou (Taiwan)
v.s: Sun Quan, hoping to find the legendary islands of Yizhou (modern Taiwan) and Danzhou (possibly Japan), sent Wei Wen and Zhuge Zhi with 10,000 armed men in large ships. The fleet reached Yizhou and spent a year there, making contact with the native inhabitants, but 'more than eight or nine out of ten soldiers died of disease' and could not stay long. This was one of the earliest large‑scale official naval expeditions in Chinese history and an early recorded instance of significant contact between mainland China and Taiwan.

v.t: Cao Wei's Grand Marshal Cao Zhen invades Shu, but heavy rains wash away the plank roads; the campaign ends in failure
v.s: To retaliate for Zhuge Liang's northern expeditions, Emperor Ming of Wei ordered Cao Zhen to lead a large army via the Ziwu Valley, Sima Yi via the Han River, and Zhang He via the Baoxie Road, a three‑pronged invasion of Shu. After Cao Zhen entered the Ziwu Valley, he encountered more than thirty consecutive days of rain; the plank roads were washed away, and supplies could not get through. Emperor Ming of Wei ordered a withdrawal. This campaign cost Wei dearly, and Shu Han took advantage to recapture the Wudu and Yinping commanderies.

v.t: Wei general Zhang He is killed by a crossbow bolt while pursuing the Shu army at the Mumen path
v.s: During Zhuge Liang's fourth northern expedition, he and Sima Yi were locked in a standoff. When Zhuge Liang withdrew due to lack of food, Sima Yi sent Zhang He in pursuit. Zhang He followed to the Mumen path (modern Li County, Gansu), where Shu troops from the high ground loosed thousands of crossbow bolts; Zhang He was hit in the right knee and died. Zhang He was one of the 'Five Elite Generals' of Cao Wei, known for his 'understanding of battle terrain and tactics, skilled and clever.' His death cost Wei a key commander.

v.t: Shapur I of the Sasanian Empire succeeds his father and continues westward expansion, invading Mesopotamia
v.s: Ardashir I died and his son Shapur I succeeded. Shapur I was even more ambitious, leading a large army against Roman Mesopotamia, capturing Nisibis and Carrhae. The eastern border of the Roman Empire was in peril, and Emperor Severus Alexander again organized a defense. The long‑running Roman–Sasanian war entered a new phase.

v.t: Zhuge Liang's fourth northern expedition: uses wooden oxen for transport, confronts Sima Yi
v.s: Zhuge Liang again led his army out of Mount Qi, using 'wooden oxen' (a type of wheelbarrow) to transport grain, solving the problem of moving supplies through mountainous terrain. Wei sent Sima Yi to replace Cao Zhen as commander of the Guanzhong forces. Sima Yi adopted a strategy of building strong walls and refusing to fight; no matter how the Shu army challenged him, even when they sent him women's clothes to humiliate him, he would not come out. The two armies faced each other at Lucheng for months. Zhuge Liang again withdrew for lack of food. In this battle, Sima Yi established the principle of 'not pursuing' against the Shu forces.

v.t: Roman emperor Severus Alexander counterattacks the Sasanian Persians and wins some victories
v.s: Severus Alexander personally led a large army east and launched a counteroffensive against the Sasanians along the Syrian and Armenian fronts. The Roman legions recovered some lost territory but could not penetrate deep into Persia. Eventually the two sides concluded a peace treaty, with Rome maintaining its defensive positions in Mesopotamia. Alexander's preference for peaceful resolution of problems displeased the war‑hungry elements in the army.

v.t: Chen Si Wang (Prince of Chen Si) Cao Zhi dies in depression, aged 41
v.s: Under the reigns of Cao Pi and Cao Rui, Cao Zhi was deeply suspected and repeatedly moved from one fief to another, never able to participate in government. He died of illness in 232 in Chen commandery (modern Huaiyang, Henan). Cao Zhi was a representative of the Jian'an literary circle; his works such as 'The Nymph of the Luo River' and 'To Prince Biao of Baima' are masterpieces. As a son of Cao Cao, he had great political talent that was never allowed to develop, but he achieved great literary heights.

v.t: Zhuge Liang rests and refurbishes in Hanzhong, improving weapons and making repeating crossbows and wooden oxen/flowing horses
v.s: After his successive northern expeditions, Zhuge Liang used a period of rest to drill his forces and improve his equipment in Hanzhong. He improved the repeating crossbow, making it capable of firing ten bolts at once; improved the grain transport vehicles, creating 'flowing horses' (another type of cart). He also implemented military‑agricultural colonies in Hanzhong to store grain. These preparations provided the material basis for the next northern expedition.

v.t: Roman emperor Severus Alexander concludes peace with the Sasanians, maintaining peace in the east
v.s: After years of war, both Rome and Sasanian Persia were exhausted. Emperor Severus Alexander and Shapur I signed a peace treaty: Rome recognized Persian control over parts of Armenia, and Persia gave up further claims on Mesopotamia. The peace lasted several years, but Roman soldiers thought the emperor too weak.

v.t: Zhuge Liang implements military‑agricultural colonies south of the Wei River, storing grain for the fifth northern expedition
v.s: To solve the problem of transporting grain for the Shu army, Zhuge Liang established military‑agricultural colonies south of the Wei River, where soldiers cultivated land and recruited refugees. This reduced the burden of transporting grain from Shu and allowed the Shu army to remain in the war zone for long periods. It was a practical application of Zhuge Liang's strategy of 'sustaining war by war'.

v.t: Germanic tribes cross the Rhine, threatening the Roman province of Gaul
v.s: The Alamanni, Franks, and other Germanic tribes massed along the Rhine, and in some places Roman border defenses had become ineffective. The Germans crossed the Rhine, plundering villages in eastern Gaul, and the Roman army was forced to contract its defense lines. These invasions were a harbinger of the collapse of the empire's western frontier during the Crisis of the Third Century.

v.t: After the Shu army withdraws, Wei Yan and Yang Yi turn on each other; Wei Yan is killed
v.s: After Zhuge Liang's death, his final orders were for Yang Yi and Fei Yi to lead the army back, with Wei Yan to cover the rear. Wei Yan refused to withdraw, burned the plank roads to block the army, and accused Yang Yi of treason. Yang Yi and Wei Yan each accused the other of rebellion. Wei Yan's men scattered, and he was killed by Ma Dai while fleeing to Hanzhong; his family was exterminated. Yang Yi later spoke resentfully and was reduced to common status. The death of Wei Yan cost Shu Han a fierce general and intensified internal conflicts.

v.t: Zhuge Liang dies of illness at the Wu Zhang Plains camp, aged 54
v.s: During his fifth northern expedition, Zhuge Liang faced Sima Yi at Wu Zhang Plains (modern Qishan, Shaanxi) for more than a hundred days. He implemented military‑agricultural colonies, preparing for a long war, but overwork led to illness and he died in camp. On his deathbed, he ordered Yang Yi, Jiang Wei, and others to keep the death secret and withdraw to Shu. Zhuge Liang's death was a great loss to Shu Han, and his northern expedition plans were laid aside. Liu Shan posthumously honored him as Marquis Zhongwu; later generations respectfully call him 'Marquis Wu'.

v.t: Zhuge Liang's fifth northern expedition: exits the Xie Valley, encamps at Wu Zhang Plains
v.s: Zhuge Liang led 100,000 troops out of the Xie Valley and encamped at Wu Zhang Plains, where he faced Sima Yi. He repeatedly challenged Sima Yi to battle, but Sima Yi would not come out. Zhuge Liang sent women's clothing to humiliate him, but still Sima Yi did not move. In August, Zhuge Liang died of illness, and the Shu army withdrew. This was Zhuge Liang's last northern expedition and the largest.

v.t: Roman emperor Severus Alexander killed by mutinous soldiers at Mainz; Severan dynasty ends
v.s: While fighting the Germans on the Rhine front, Severus Alexander tried to buy them off with money rather than force, causing dissatisfaction in the army. A group of mutinous soldiers burst into the emperor's tent and killed him and his mother. They then proclaimed the Thracian officer Maximinus as emperor. Alexander's death marked the end of the Severan dynasty, and Rome entered the chaotic 'military anarchy' phase of the Crisis of the Third Century (26 emperors in 50 years, almost all proclaimed and killed by the army).

v.t: Jiang Wan takes charge of Shu Han affairs after Zhuge Liang, continuing the plans for northern expeditions
v.s: After Zhuge Liang's death, Liu Shan, following his will, appointed Jiang Wan as Minister of the Masses, in charge of state affairs. Jiang Wan was the successor trained by Zhuge Liang, steady and prudent; he continued Zhuge Liang's policies of rest and military preparation. He promoted new figures such as Fei Yi and Jiang Wei, maintaining Shu Han's stability.

v.t: Emperor Ming of Wei (Cao Rui) embarks on lavish construction of Luoyang palaces, depleting state resources
v.s: In his later years, Emperor Ming of Wei embarked on extensive construction of palaces in Luoyang, including the Zhaoyang and Taiji halls, elaborately decorated. He recruited many laborers, spent huge sums, and even followed the advice of Gao Tanglong to 'grandly build palace chambers.' Ministers such as Yang Fu and Gao Tanglong repeatedly remonstrated, but the emperor ignored them. This ruinous expenditure exacerbated social conflicts.

v.t: Roman Empire plunges into the Crisis of the Third Century; military anarchy begins
v.s: After Severus Alexander was killed, the Roman Empire entered a full‑blown crisis lasting fifty years. The silver content of the denarius fell to 5%, inflation spiraled out of control; the army deposed and appointed emperors at will, and separatist regimes emerged in the provinces (the Gallic Empire and Palmyrene Empire); the Goths, Alamanni, Franks, and Sasanian Persians simultaneously invaded all frontiers. Over the next fifty years, twenty‑six emperors were proclaimed, most ruling only a few months before being murdered. The Roman Empire was on the verge of collapse until Diocletian came to power in 284 and implemented comprehensive reforms to restore order.

v.t: Conflict intensifies between the Roman Senate and the army‑proclaimed emperor Maximinus
v.s: Maximinus Thrax, who had never been to Rome, ruled only with the support of the army. To pay his enormous military expenses, he confiscated aristocratic property on a large scale, provoking Senate anger. The Senate secretly contacted the African garrisons, planning to overthrow Maximinus. The conflict between the Roman center and the provincial armies worsened the civil war.

v.t: Gordian I proclaimed emperor in Africa by the Senate, then commits suicide after defeat
v.s: The Senate proclaimed the elderly Gordian I and his son Gordian II as emperors in Africa to oppose Maximinus. Maximinus sent an army to attack; Gordian II was killed in battle, and Gordian I hanged himself. The Senate then proclaimed Decius as emperor. In the course of a year, several emperors were killed, and the political situation was extremely chaotic.

v.t: Roman emperor Gordian III forced to make peace with Sasanian Persia to focus on internal rebellions
v.s: Gordian III was proclaimed emperor by the Senate at the age of 13. With the empire under attack on all sides, he was forced to make peace with Sasanian Persia, paying an annual tribute to secure the eastern frontier. Meanwhile, the Roman army needed to suppress rebellions in Africa and Gaul. This marked a posture of general retreat for the Roman Empire amid the Crisis of the Third Century.

v.t: Gongsun Yuan declares himself King of Yan, openly rebelling against Cao Wei
v.s: The governor of Liaodong, Gongsun Yuan, had long been a de facto independent ruler, secretly in contact with Eastern Wu. In 237, he declared himself King of Yan, changed the era name to Shaohan, set up a bureaucracy, and openly broke with Cao Wei. He sent envoys to the Xianbei, hoping to use external forces to oppose Wei. Emperor Ming of Wei decided to send Sima Yi to lead an army against him.

v.t: Rebellion breaks out in the Roman province of Africa; conflict between Senate and army intensifies
v.s: In the course of suppressing the African rebellion, Maximinus used brutal methods against civilians and senators, causing uprisings in many parts of North Africa. At the same time, Goths from the Danube frontier invaded, taking advantage of the chaos. The entire empire was caught in the double crisis of civil war and foreign invasion.

v.t: Queen Himiko of Yamatai in Japan sends envoys to Cao Wei, receives title 'Pro‑Wei King of Wa'
v.s: Himiko, the queen of the Yamatai kingdom in the Japanese archipelago (located perhaps in Kyushu or Nara), was a shamaness who ruled over more than thirty tribes. She sent envoys – Nanshengmi and Dushiniuli – via the Korean peninsula to Luoyang, offering male and female slaves and striped cloth. Emperor Ming of Wei granted Himiko a gold seal with the title 'Pro‑Wei King of Wa', and bestowed large amounts of silk and bronze mirrors. This is an important record of early Sino‑Japanese relations, found in the 'Wei Annals' of the 'Records of the Three Kingdoms'.

v.t: Sima Yi leads army to pacify Liaodong, killing Gongsun Yuan
v.s: Sima Yi led 40,000 troops against Gongsun Yuan. Using a feint, he bypassed the main enemy positions and marched directly to Xiangping (modern Liaoyang, Liaoning). Gongsun Yuan called for help from the Xianbei and Goguryeo, but Sima Yi blocked them. The Wei army besieged the city for three months, and when food ran out, Gongsun Yuan tried to flee but was captured. Sima Yi executed Gongsun Yuan and his son, and the four commanderies of Liaodong, Xuantu, Lelang, and Daifang came under Cao Wei control.

v.t: Goths, allied with other tribes, again invade Roman territory on a large scale, plundering the Balkans
v.s: The Goths, together with the Carpi and other tribes, crossed the Danube into the Roman provinces of Dacia and Moesia. They besieged Marcianopolis, plundered villages, and even threatened Greek cities on the Black Sea coast. The Roman army, under‑strength, could only defend passively.

v.t: Emperor Ming of Wei (Cao Rui) dies; Sima Yi and Cao Shuang appointed regents
v.s: Emperor Ming of Wei died in Luoyang at the age of 36. On his deathbed, he summoned Sima Yi and Cao Shuang and appointed them as regents for the eight‑year‑old crown prince Cao Fang. Cao Shuang became Grand General, and Sima Yi became Grand Commandant, sharing power. The emperor's early death left a power vacuum in Cao Wei politics, and the Sima family began to covet supreme power.

v.t: Cao Shuang and Sima Yi serve as co‑regents, but Cao Shuang monopolizes power and excludes Sima Yi
v.s: Cao Shuang used his authority as Grand General to promote Sima Yi to the honorary post of Grand Commandant, stripping him of military command. He relied on his cronies He Yan, Deng Yang, Ding Mi and others, monopolizing the government and excluding Sima Yi. Sima Yi, sidelined, feigned illness and stayed at home, secretly waiting for an opportunity. Cao Shuang's policies angered many ministers.

v.t: Roman civil wars see multiple emperors; chronological chaos
v.s: In the year 238, six emperors were successively proclaimed (Maximinus, Gordian I, Gordian II, Pupienus, Balbinus, Gordian III), all killed or committing suicide. The year 238 is known as the 'Year of the Six Emperors'. Thereafter the Roman dating system fell into chaos, with local warlords reckoning years independently.

v.t: Teenage Roman emperor Gordian III mysteriously dies during an eastern campaign
v.s: Gordian III, proclaimed emperor at age 13, died fighting the Sasanian Persians (or was killed) in 244. His successor, Philip the Arab, quickly concluded a peace with Persia, recognizing Persian control of Armenia. Roman prestige in the east was greatly diminished.

v.t: Roman emperor Philip the Arab persecutes Christians, closes churches
v.s: Philip the Arab was the first emperor with recorded Christian sympathies, but he also permitted persecution of Christians. In Rome, some Christians were executed for refusing to participate in pagan rituals. Christianity remained illegal in the Roman Empire, and believers had to meet in secret.

v.t: The Mahavihara monastery in Sri Lanka is established, becoming a center of Theravada Buddhism
v.s: The king of Sri Lanka built the Mahavihara at Anuradhapura, which became an important monastery for Theravada Buddhism. The Mahavihara school used the Pali language for its scriptures, which later spread to Myanmar, Thailand, Laos, and other countries, forming the Southeast Asian Theravada Buddhist cultural sphere.

v.t: Cao Shuang leads an army to attack Shu, but is defeated by Shu generals Fei Yi and Wang Ping
v.s: To gain military glory, Cao Shuang led 100,000 men from the Luo Valley into Shu. The Shu Han commander of Hanzhong, Wang Ping, held the strategic Mount Xingshi strongpoint, and Fei Yi brought reinforcements from Fuxian. After a long siege and shortage of supplies, many Wei soldiers fell ill. Fei Yi cut off their retreat, and the Wei army was badly defeated, losing tens of thousands. Cao Shuang's prestige fell sharply.

v.t: Philip the Arab is proclaimed Roman emperor by the army
v.s: After the death of Gordian III on the Persian front, his Praetorian prefect, Philip the Arab, was proclaimed emperor by the army. He quickly made peace with the Sasanian Persians and returned to Rome, where he was recognized by the Senate. During his reign, he celebrated the 1000th anniversary of the founding of Rome.

v.t: He Yan and Wang Bi initiate Xuanxue (Neo‑Daoism); the fashion for 'pure conversation' begins among scholar‑officials
