v.s: During the Zhengshi era (240–249) of Cao Wei, scholar‑officials such as He Yan and Wang Bi studied the 'Three Mysteries' (Laozi, Zhuangzi, and the Book of Changes), exploring philosophical concepts such as 'being and non‑being', 'root and branch', and 'substance and function'. Wang Bi, at age 20, wrote a commentary on the Laozi, proposing a metaphysics of 'non‑being as the root' and founding the 'non‑being' school of Xuanxue. The rise of Xuanxue reflected the desire of scholar‑officials to escape reality and seek spiritual freedom under political oppression, deeply influencing the thought and culture of the Six Dynasties period.

v.t: Eastern Wu's Quan Cong attacks Wei, is defeated
v.s: Eastern Wu generals Quan Cong and Zhu Huan led tens of thousands of troops to attack Wei's Huainan region, besieging Fancheng. Wei's General Who Conquers the East, Wang Ling, and Inspector of Yangzhou, Sun Li, organized a defense. The Wu attack failed, and a plague broke out, forcing them to withdraw. Neither side achieved a decisive victory.

v.t: Shapur I of the Sasanian Empire succeeds his father and continues his expansionist policies
v.s: Ardashir I died, and his son Shapur I became emperor of Sasanian Persia. He immediately set about consolidating the eastern frontier, conquering parts of the Kushan Empire, then turned west to prepare for an attack on the Roman Empire. Over the next twenty years, Shapur I would repeatedly defeat Roman armies and become one of Rome's most formidable opponents.

v.t: Sasanian Persian army occupies western Armenia, making it a client state
v.s: Shapur I sent troops into Armenia, defeating the pro‑Roman faction there and installing a pro‑Persian king. The kingdom of Armenia became a buffer between the Sasanians and Rome, but was in effect under Persian control. The Roman Empire, preoccupied with internal strife, was unable to intervene.

v.t: Goths invade the Roman heartland for the first time, plundering the Danube banks
v.s: Gothic tribal confederations crossed the Danube, penetrating deep into the provinces of Dacia and Moesia, destroying Roman cities along the way. The Roman garrison could not resist, and Emperor Decius led a campaign in person. This marked the beginning of the Goths as a major threat to Rome.

v.t: Wei's Yangzhou commander Wang Ling promotes military colonies in Huainan, storing grain
v.s: Wei's General Who Conquers the East, Wang Ling, vigorously promoted military‑agricultural colonies in the Huainan region, repairing hydraulic works such as the Shaobei reservoir and cultivating wasteland, storing millions of hu of grain in preparation for military action against Eastern Wu. The colonies strengthened Wei's border defenses, but also gave Wang Ling considerable military and economic autonomy in Huainan.

v.t: Shu Han's Grand General Jiang Wan plans a river‑borne invasion of Wei from the Han and Mian rivers, but does not implement it
v.s: Jiang Wan believed that the route via Mount Qi, which Zhuge Liang had repeatedly used, was too difficult and that it would be better to go downstream on the Han and Mian rivers to attack Wei's Shangyong and Weixing regions. He began building large warships, but many ministers thought the river route risky, and Fei Yi, Jiang Wei, and others expressed opposition. Jiang Wan shelved the plan.

v.t: Sima Yi feigns old age and illness to deceive Cao Shuang, secretly contacting his confidants
v.s: Seeing that Cao Shuang was abusing his power, Sima Yi claimed to be ill and took no part in politics. When Cao Shuang sent men to check on him, Sima Yi acted senile and incoherent. Cao Shuang believed him and let down his guard. Sima Yi, meanwhile, secretly contacted old ministers such as Jiang Ji and Gao Rou, preparing a coup.

v.t: Fei Yi becomes Grand General of Shu Han, continuing Jiang Wan's policy of rest and recuperation
v.s: Jiang Wan, ill, handed state affairs over to Fei Yi. Fei Yi became Grand General and Minister over the Masses. He continued Zhuge Liang's policies, developing the economy at home and adopting a defensive posture externally, reducing the consumption of resources for northern expeditions. He believed that Shu Han's national strength was limited and that frequent military campaigns were inadvisable, so he placed restrictions on Jiang Wei's proposals for northern expeditions.

v.t: Cao Shuang, monopolizing power, lives extravagantly and alienates the court, causing popular discontent
v.s: Cao Shuang, as Grand General, controlled state affairs, allowed his cronies He Yan and Deng Yang to embezzle, and even kept the late Emperor Ming's concubines for himself. He undertook grand construction projects and lived luxuriously, in stark contrast to Sima Yi's self‑effacement. Many officials were disappointed with him and inclined to side with Sima Yi.

v.t: Roman emperor Philip the Arab is forced to abdicate and killed by his subordinate Decius
v.s: Philip the Arab sent Decius to suppress a rebellion on the Danube, but Decius was proclaimed emperor by the troops. Philip led his army to meet him but was killed at the Battle of Verona. Decius became Roman emperor. He was an Illyrian provincial and the first soldier‑emperor to rise amid the barbarian invasions.

v.t: Eastern Wu digs the Pogandu Canal, linking the Qinhuai River and the Lake Tai basin
v.s: Eastern Wu dug the Pogandu Canal (in modern Jurong, Jiangsu) south of Jianye, connecting the Qinhuai River with the Lake Tai basin, making it easier for shipping to move around the region. The canal facilitated commercial circulation within Eastern Wu and allowed Jianye to receive grain supplies from the Lake Tai region. This was an important early engineering project for the Jiangnan canal system.

v.t: Roman emperor Decius orders universal sacrifice, launching a large‑scale persecution of Christians
v.s: Roman emperor Decius issued an edict ordering all inhabitants to sacrifice to the Roman gods under the supervision of local officials and obtain a certificate (libellus). Those who refused faced imprisonment, confiscation, or death. Many Christians were martyred, including Pope Fabian of Rome and Bishop Alexander of Jerusalem. This was the first organized, systematic persecution of Christianity in the Roman Empire.

v.t: Roman emperor Philip the Arab makes peace with Sasanian Persia, confirming existing borders
v.s: After a long standoff with Persia, Philip the Arab decided to make peace. He recognized Sasanian control over parts of Armenia and Mesopotamia and paid a huge indemnity to secure peace on the eastern frontier. This 'peace for money' approach aroused discontent among the Roman populace and army.

v.t: Queen Yi‑yo of Yamatai sends envoys to Cao Wei
v.s: After the death of Queen Himiko of Yamatai, there was a civil war. She was succeeded by her relative Yi‑yo. Yi‑yo sent envoys to Luoyang, offering white pearls and green jade. The Wei court granted her a title and seal. Thereafter, official contacts between Yamatai and China gradually diminished.

v.t: Roman emperor Decius is killed at the Battle of Abritus
v.s: Decius led his army against the invading Goths and was trapped with his army in the swamps of Abritus on the lower Danube, unable to form a proper battle line. Decius and his son Herennius Etruscus were both killed, making him the first Roman emperor to die fighting a foreign enemy. The Goths, emboldened, raided Roman territory even more freely.

v.t: Rome holds a millennial celebration – but actually only 998 years since founding
v.s: To win popular support, Roman emperor Philip the Arab held a celebration of the 1000th anniversary of the founding of Rome. According to Varro's calculation, Rome was founded in 753 BC, so 247 AD would be 1000 years later. Actually it was 998 years, but Philip nonetheless held a magnificent celebration, including gladiatorial games, theatrical performances, and religious ceremonies. It was the last large‑scale traditional celebration of the Roman Empire.

v.t: Roman general Gallus succeeds, making peace with the Goths
v.s: After Decius was killed, the Roman army proclaimed Gallus as emperor. Gallus immediately made peace with the Goths, allowing them to keep their booty and agreeing to pay them an annual tribute. The humiliating peace further undermined Roman prestige on the frontier.

v.t: Goths again cross the Danube and invade the Roman Balkan provinces
v.s: Seeing the weakness of the new emperor Gallus, the Goths again invaded Moesia and Thrace in force. They besieged Marcianopolis and plundered Philippopolis (modern Plovdiv, Bulgaria), taking many inhabitants as slaves. The Roman army, crippled by civil war, could not mount an effective counterattack.

v.t: A severe plague ravages the Roman Empire, causing massive loss of life
v.s: At the same time as war and famine, a plague swept the Roman Empire, probably brought back by troops returning from the east. The plague spread through the Balkans and Italy, and the populations of Rome and army camps were greatly reduced. Emperor Gallus was unable to cope, and the empire sank deeper into crisis.

v.t: Rebellion of minority tribes breaks out on Shu Han's Liangzhou border; Jiang Wei leads army to pacify it
v.s: In the Liangzhou area under Shu Han control (southern Gansu), some Qiang chieftains allied with Wei and rebelled. Jiang Wei led his army west and, after months of fighting, crushed the rebellion, recaptured lost territory, and incorporated some surrendered Qiang tribes into the Shu army. This campaign honed Jiang Wei's command skills.

v.t: Conflict between Sima Yi and Cao Shuang intensifies; a coup is imminent
v.s: Cao Shuang moved Empress Dowager Guo to the Yongning Palace, further tightening his control over the government. Sima Yi, in secret, contacted commanders of the imperial guard and other old hands of Cao Wei, preparing a coup. Both sides were waiting for the right moment, and a dramatic power struggle was about to erupt.

v.t: Goths besiege Marcianopolis in the Balkans but fail to take it
v.s: The Goths again besieged Marcianopolis (modern Bulgaria), but the Roman garrison resisted stubbornly. The Goths lacked siege equipment and withdrew after several months. Nonetheless, the countryside and towns of the Balkans had already suffered severe damage.

v.t: Goths continue raiding Roman borders and plunder the Aegean coast
v.s: The Goths built ships and attacked Roman ports via the Black Sea coast, even threatening the Greek peninsula. The Roman navy was in decline and could not prevent these sea raids. Gothic pirate activity extended to the northern coast of Asia Minor.

v.t: Roman emperor Gallus is overthrown by his subordinate Aemilianus and then killed
v.s: The Roman general Aemilianus was proclaimed emperor by his troops in Moesia and marched on Italy. Emperor Gallus sent troops to stop him but was defeated and killed. However, Aemilianus reigned only three months before being overthrown by another general, Valerian.

v.t: Sima Yi launches the Coup of the Gaoping Tombs, eliminates Cao Shuang's faction, and monopolizes Wei power
v.s: Cao Shuang accompanied the emperor Cao Fang on a visit to the Gaoping Tombs (the mausoleum of Emperor Ming of Wei). Taking advantage of their absence, Sima Yi staged a coup, closed the gates of Luoyang, and seized the arsenals and military camps. In the name of the Empress Dowager, he accused Cao Shuang of treason and sent men to persuade him to surrender. After hesitating, Cao Shuang laid down his arms. Soon after, Sima Yi executed Cao Shuang, his associates He Yan and Deng Yang, and their three clans (thousands of people). From then on, the Cao Wei regime effectively fell into the hands of the Sima clan.

v.t: After Emperor Decius dies at the Battle of Abrittus, the army proclaims Gallus as emperor
v.s: Emperor Decius was killed in battle against the Goths at Abrittus (modern Razgrad, Bulgaria) in June 251. After his death, the Roman army in Moesia proclaimed Trebonianus Gallus as emperor. Gallus quickly made peace with the Goths, allowing them to leave with their plunder, and then turned his attention to the plague and barbarian invasions that were devastating the empire. Decius was the first Roman emperor to be killed in battle by a foreign enemy.

v.t: Valerian is proclaimed Roman emperor, ending the brief period of chaos
v.s: After Aemilianus was killed, the Danubian legions proclaimed Valerian as emperor. Valerian, of senatorial rank, immediately marched to Rome, was recognized by the Senate, and appointed his son Gallienus as co‑emperor. Valerian attempted to restore order, but the empire was already in a state of general crisis.

v.t: After the Gaoping coup, Sima Yi begins to purge Cao Shuang's allies
v.s: After the coup, Sima Yi ordered the arrest of Cao Shuang, He Yan, Deng Yang, Ding Mi, and their extended families, all of whom were executed. He also dismissed many officials promoted by Cao Shuang and replaced them with his own trusted men. From then on, the Sima faction completely controlled Cao Wei politics.

v.t: Sima Yi suppresses the rebellion of Wang Ling, eliminating his power base
v.s: Wei's Grand Commandant Wang Ling and his nephew, the Inspector of Yanzhou, Linghu Yu, plotted to establish the Prince of Chu, Cao Biao, as emperor and overthrow Sima Yi. When Sima Yi learned of it, he led a rapid march to Huainan. Wang Ling, knowing he was beaten, surrendered and was forced to commit suicide. Sima Yi had all his co‑conspirators executed and forced Cao Biao to commit suicide. Sima Yi further consolidated his power.

v.t: Sun Quan, due to a succession dispute, dethrones his eldest son Sun He, causing internal turmoil in Eastern Wu
v.s: In his later years, Sun Quan doted on his youngest son Sun Liang and another son, Sun Ba, while the crown prince Sun He's position became precarious. Sun Quan, listening to slander, deposed Sun He and made Sun Liang crown prince, while also killing Sun Ba. This 'dispute of the two princes' split the court of Eastern Wu, drawing in such ministers as Lu Xun and Zhu Ju. Lu Xun died of anger. The political situation in Eastern Wu became chaotic.

v.t: Teotihuacan civilization reaches its zenith; the Sun Pyramid is completed
v.s: Teotihuacan in Mesoamerica reached its peak around 250 AD, with a population of about 125,000 and an area of 21 square kilometers. The Sun Pyramid, completed at this time, stands 65 meters high with a base 225 meters on each side, the largest pyramid in Mesoamerica. By controlling obsidian trade, Teotihuacan dominated the region, and its influence extended to the Maya area.

v.t: The Maya civilization enters the Classic period; large pyramid‑temples built at Tikal, Palenque, etc.
v.s: Maya civilization in Mesoamerica entered the Classic period (250–900 AD), lasting about 650 years. Rulers of city‑states such as Tikal, Palenque, and Copán built grand pyramid‑temples, palaces, and ballcourts. The Maya calculated the solar year with accuracy to 365.242 days, independently invented the concept of zero in mathematics, and developed a full hieroglyphic writing system. This was one of the peaks of pre‑Columbian civilization in the Americas.

v.t: Roman emperor Valerian goes to war with Sasanian Persia, recovering some lost territory
v.s: Valerian resolved to restore Roman dignity in the east and led an expedition against the Sasanian Persians. The Roman legions initially achieved minor victories, recovering parts of Syria and Armenia. But the tide of war soon turned.

v.t: Roman emperor Decius launches a large‑scale persecution of Christians
v.s: To restore traditional religion, Roman emperor Decius (reigned 249–251) ordered all inhabitants to sacrifice to the Roman gods and obtain a certificate. Christians who refused were arrested and executed. Pope Fabian of Rome, Bishop Alexander of Jerusalem, and many others were martyred. This was the first organized, systematic persecution in the empire, and though it did not destroy Christianity, it showed the Roman state's hostility to the faith.

v.t: Maya Classic period begins
v.s: Circa 250–950 AD, Maya civilization entered the Classic period. The Maya built dozens of city‑states in the lowlands of Mesoamerica (modern Guatemala, Belize, Mexico's Yucatán Peninsula), including Tikal, Palenque, Copán, and Calakmul. The Maya developed a full writing system, precise calendars (the Long Count), mathematics (including the concept of zero), and astronomy. Maya pyramids, palaces, and ballcourts covered the jungles of Mesoamerica. Around 900 AD, the Classic Maya civilization collapsed, likely due to drought, warfare, and environmental degradation.

v.t: Sima Shi succeeds Sima Yi in power and reorganizes the government
v.s: After Sima Yi's death, his eldest son Sima Shi succeeded as Grand General and continued to control Wei affairs. Sima Shi suppressed opposition and consolidated the Sima clan's rule. He selected capable men, reformed administration, defended against Wu and Shu, and guarded against political enemies at home.

v.t: Wei regent Sima Yi dies; posthumous title Xuanwen Hou
v.s: Sima Yi died of illness in Luoyang at the age of 72. He had served as a senior minister under four Wei emperors. After the Gaoping coup, he effectively controlled the Wei government. His sons Sima Shi and Sima Zhao inherited his power, eventually completing the transition from Wei to Jin. Sima Yi was posthumously honored as Marquis Xuanwen, and when his grandson Sima Yan became emperor, he was honored as Emperor Xuan of Jin.

v.t: Roman emperor Decius is killed at the Battle of Abritus against the Goths
v.s: Decius led his Roman legions into a decisive battle with the Goths in the marshes of Abritus. The Roman army became bogged down, and the Goths used the terrain to their advantage, routing the Romans. Decius and his son Herennius Etruscus were both killed. This was the first time a Roman emperor died in a foreign war.

v.t: Roman emperor Valerian is routed in the east and captured by the Sasanian Persians
v.s: Valerian led his army against Sasanian Persian king Shapur I near Edessa. The Roman army, crippled by plague and shortage of supplies, was badly defeated. Valerian, trying to negotiate, was captured by the Persians. Shapur I displayed him as a captive, showing Valerian kneeling to him in rock reliefs. Valerian died in Persian captivity, the first Roman emperor to be captured by an enemy.

v.t: Goths build a fleet and attack Roman coastal cities in Asia Minor from the Black Sea
v.s: Using ships built on the Black Sea coast, the Goths launched naval attacks on the Roman Empire. They passed through the Bosporus and raided the port cities of Sinope and Trapezus in northern Asia Minor. The Gothic seaborne threat forced Rome to strengthen its navy, but with limited effect.

v.t: Sun Quan, Emperor Da of Wu, dies at the age of 71
v.s: Sun Quan died of illness in Jianye, with the posthumous name Great Emperor, buried at Jiangling. He was the longest‑lived of the three monarchs of the Three Kingdoms. During his reign, he succeeded his brother Sun Ce's foundations in Jiangdong, crushed Cao Cao at Red Cliffs, defeated Liu Bei at Xiaoting, established Eastern Wu, and expanded into Jiaozhou (modern Guangdong, Guangxi, and northern Vietnam). After his death, his son Sun Liang succeeded, at age 10, with Zhuge Ke and Sun Jun acting as regents.

v.t: Zhuge Ke, as Grand Tutor, takes charge of Eastern Wu's government, becoming a powerful regent
v.s: After Sun Quan's death, Zhuge Ke (nephew of Zhuge Liang), as Grand General and Grand Tutor to the Heir Apparent, seized control of the government. He led the Wu army to defeat a Wei invasion, raising his prestige. Then, overestimating his abilities, he planned a large‑scale northern campaign regardless of the state's resources.

v.t: Roman emperor Gallienus, after his father Valerian's capture, rules the western empire alone
v.s: After Valerian was captured by the Sasanian Persians, his son Gallienus became sole emperor of the western Roman Empire. He faced full‑scale invasions by the Goths, Franks, and Alamanni, as well as usurpers among his own generals (the period of the 'Thirty Tyrants'). Gallienus introduced military reforms, creating a mobile cavalry‑based field army to respond quickly to crises.

v.t: Roman emperor Valerian is captured by Sasanian Persia and made a footstool for the Persian king
v.s: Valerian was disastrously defeated at the Battle of Edessa and captured by Shapur I. According to Roman sources, Shapur I used Valerian as a footstool, stepping on his back to mount his horse. Valerian was kept as a slave until his death. Shapur I depicted Valerian kneeling in supplication in reliefs at Naqsh‑e Rostam to celebrate his triumph.

v.t: All Roman frontiers are in crisis; rebellions and invasions break out simultaneously in Gaul, Pannonia, and Syria
v.s: When news of Valerian's capture spread, chaos erupted throughout the Roman Empire. In Gaul, Postumus proclaimed himself emperor, establishing the Gallic Empire. In the east, Odaenathus of Palmyra remained nominally loyal but was effectively independent. In the Danube region, the Goths ran rampant. The Roman Empire was on the brink of collapse and fragmentation.

v.t: Eastern Wu regent Zhuge Ke leads 200,000 troops north and suffers a disastrous defeat at Xincheng
v.s: Against the advice of many, Zhuge Ke mobilized 200,000 provincial troops for a massive northern expedition against Wei, besieging Hefei Xincheng (modern Hefei, Anhui). The Wei commander Zhang Te stubbornly defended, and after a long siege combined with summer heat and plague, more than half of the Wu army died. Zhuge Ke was forced to retreat, losing all his prestige.

v.t: Shu Han Grand General Fei Yi is assassinated by a surrendered Wei general
v.s: Fei Yi was hosting a New Year's feast at Hanshou (modern Jiange, Sichuan) when he was stabbed to death by Guo Xun (or Guo Xiu), a surrendered Wei officer. Guo Xun had been captured by Jiang Wei during a northern campaign and had surrendered, but he secretly remained loyal to Wei. Fei Yi's death removed the main check on Jiang Wei, who thereafter launched frequent northern expeditions.

v.t: Roman emperor Gallienus institutes military reforms, creating a mobile field army based on cavalry
v.s: Gallienus realized that traditional Roman legions lacked mobility and could not cope with multiple fronts. He created a mobile field army (Comitatenses) composed mainly of cavalry and light infantry, based at Milan, to rapidly reinforce threatened areas. He also promoted officers of Illyrian origin, who later became the core of Rome's revival.

v.t: Sima Shi deposes Emperor Cao Fang and installs Cao Mao as emperor
v.s: Wei emperor Cao Fang plotted with the Palace Attendant Li Feng and Grand Commandant Xiahou Xuan to kill Sima Shi. The plot was discovered. Sima Shi executed Li Feng, Xiahou Xuan, and others, then, in the name of the Empress Dowager, deposed Cao Fang, demoting him to Prince of Qi. He proclaimed the Duke of Gaogui, Cao Mao, as emperor (the fourth emperor of Cao Wei). Sima Shi continued to control the government.

v.t: Franks invade Gaul, destroying Roman border defenses
v.s: The Franks, a confederation of Germanic tribes from the lower Rhine, took advantage of the Roman civil war to break through the Rhine frontier, plundering northern Gaul and devastating cities such as Cologne and Trier. Roman rule in Gaul was severely threatened.

v.t: Eastern Wu regent Sun Jun kills Zhuge Ke and takes control
v.s: After Zhuge Ke's failed northern campaign and loss of prestige, Emperor Sun Liang conspired with the imperial clan member Sun Jun to have Zhuge Ke murdered at a banquet and exterminate his family. Sun Jun was promoted to Chancellor, Grand General, and Commander of all military affairs, monopolizing power. Sun Jun's arrogance aroused discontent at court and in the provinces.

v.t: Roman emperor Gallienus repulses Alamanni invasion of northern Italy
v.s: The Alamanni crossed the Alps and entered northern Italy, threatening Milan and Rome. Gallienus led his mobile field army to meet them and defeated the Alamanni near Milan. This was the first time in decades that a Roman army had beaten a barbarian force on Italian soil, and it somewhat boosted morale.

v.t: Sima Shi suppresses the rebellion of Guanqiu Jian and Wen Qin, then dies; Sima Zhao takes power
v.s: Wei's General Who Guards the East, Guanqiu Jian, and Inspector of Yangzhou, Wen Qin, raised troops to overthrow Sima Shi. Despite being ill, Sima Shi personally led the campaign and crushed them. Guanqiu Jian was killed, and Wen Qin fled to Eastern Wu. Sima Shi died of illness on the way back to Xuchang. His brother Sima Zhao succeeded as Grand General, continuing to control the government.

v.t: Shu Han's Jiang Wei conducts a northern expedition, defeating Wei general Wang Jing at Didao
v.s: Jiang Wei led several tens of thousands of troops north. The Wei Inspector of Yongzhou, Wang Jing, met him in battle. At Taoxi (modern Lintao, Gansu), Jiang Wei inflicted a major defeat on the Wei army, with tens of thousands killed. Wang Jing retreated to Didao (modern Lintao, Gansu). Wei sent Chen Tai and Deng Ai with reinforcements, and Jiang Wei withdrew when his supplies ran out. This was the most successful of Jiang Wei's northern expeditions.

v.t: Guanqiu Jian and Wen Qin rise in Huainan against Sima Shi, are defeated and killed
v.s: Under the slogan 'overthrow the traitor Sima Shi', Guanqiu Jian and Wen Qin raised troops at Shouchun, but most people in Huainan did not respond. Sima Shi led an army to suppress them and routed the rebels. Guanqiu Jian fled but was captured and killed; Wen Qin fled to Eastern Wu. The second of the three Huainan rebellions was put down.

v.t: Sasanian Shapur I captures the Roman city of Antioch, deporting its population
v.s: Taking advantage of Valerian's capture, Shapur I led his forces into the Roman province of Syria and occupied Antioch, the third largest city in the Roman Empire. The Persian army sacked the city and carried off many artisans, scholars, and slaves, settling them in the new Persian city of Gundeshapur, where they contributed to Persian cultural and technological development.

v.t: After his victory at Taoxi, Jiang Wei besieges Didao but is repelled
v.s: Following his great victory at Taoxi, Jiang Wei attempted to capture Didao. Wei generals Deng Ai and Chen Tai arrived with reinforcements. Jiang Wei failed to take the city and, hearing that Wei forces were about to attack his rear, withdrew. Although Jiang Wei had won a victory, he did not gain any territory.

v.t: Eastern Wu regent Sun Jun dies; his cousin Sun Chen seizes power
v.s: After Sun Jun's death, his cousin Sun Chen became Grand General and became even more despotic. Emperor Sun Liang, dissatisfied with Sun Chen, tried to regain power but was controlled by Sun Chen. Sun Chen deposed Sun Liang and installed Sun Xiu, Sun Quan's sixth son, as emperor, making himself Chancellor.

v.t: The Gallic Empire formally secedes from Rome, controlling Gaul, Britain, and Spain
v.s: The Roman general Postumus proclaimed himself emperor in Gaul, establishing the 'Gallic Empire' comprising Gaul, Britain, and Spain, with its capital at Cologne. Gallienus, focused on defending Italy and the Balkans, temporarily recognized the Gallic Empire's existence. The Roman Empire was effectively divided into three parts: the central Roman state, the Gallic Empire, and the Palmyrene kingdom in the east.

v.t: Roman emperor Valerian issues an edict again ordering persecution of Christians, executing bishops and clergy
v.s: Before his capture, Valerian had ordered the persecution of Christians, requiring all bishops and clergy to sacrifice to the Roman gods or be exiled or executed. Pope Sixtus II, Saint Lawrence, and many others were martyred. A large amount of church property was confiscated. This persecution continued until Valerian's capture.

v.t: Wei's General Who Conquers the East, Zhuge Dan, raises troops at Shouchun against Sima Zhao
v.s: Zhuge Dan (a distant cousin of Zhuge Liang), as General Who Conquers the East, garrisoned Huainan. Fearing that Sima Zhao would attack him, he rebelled and sought help from Eastern Wu. Zhuge Dan sent his son as a hostage to Wu, and the state of Wu sent Wen Qin and Quan Yi with 30,000 troops to relieve Shouchun. This was the third of the three Huainan rebellions.

v.t: The Gallic Empire, under Postumus, consolidates its territory and issues its own coinage
v.s: Postumus established effective administration in Gaul and issued gold coins bearing his own portrait. The Gallic Empire possessed strong legions (most of the former Rhine garrisons), which protected Gaul and Britain from barbarian incursions. In fact, it defended the western frontier more effectively than the central Roman government.

v.t: Sima Zhao, after a long siege, captures Shouchun and kills Zhuge Dan
v.s: Sima Zhao personally led 260,000 troops to surround Shouchun. After months of siege, the city ran out of food. Zhuge Dan killed Wen Qin, causing an internal collapse. The Wu relief force was also defeated. When the city fell, Zhuge Dan was killed and his three clans were exterminated. With the three Huainan rebellions now fully suppressed, Sima Zhao eliminated all internal opposition in Cao Wei.

v.t: Eastern Wu's Sun Chen deposes Sun Liang and installs Sun Xiu; Sun Xiu then kills Sun Chen
v.s: Sun Chen deposed Sun Liang, making him Marquis of Kuaiji, and welcomed Sun Xiu (Sun Quan's sixth son) as emperor (Emperor Jing of Wu). Sun Xiu initially bided his time, then, together with Zhang Bu and Ding Feng, killed Sun Chen at a sacrifice ceremony. Sun Xiu began ruling in person, and the political situation in Eastern Wu temporarily stabilized.

v.t: Sasanian Persian Shapur I captures Roman emperor Valerian at the Battle of Edessa
v.s: Sasanian Persian king Shapur I annihilated the Roman army near Edessa and captured Emperor Valerian. Shapur forced Valerian to kneel before him and later held him prisoner for life. This was one of the most shameful defeats in Roman history. The Sasanian Empire reached its greatest territorial extent.

v.t: Roman emperor Gallienus successfully resists the Persians in the east, recovering some lost territory
v.s: Although Gallienus could not campaign in person in the east, he appointed the capable Odaenathus as commander of the Roman forces in the East. Odaenathus gathered the remaining Roman troops and Palmyrene cavalry, repelled further Sasanian attacks, and recaptured some cities in Mesopotamia, stabilizing the eastern front.

v.t: The Palmyrene kingdom rises under Odaenathus, becoming a power in the east
v.s: Odaenathus, a noble of Palmyra (modern Syria), had been appointed by Rome as 'Commander of the East' (Corrector Orientis). Taking advantage of Rome's weakness, he built his own military forces. He conquered Syria, Mesopotamia, and other regions, becoming effectively independent while still nominally loyal to Rome. His wife Zenobia later founded a fully independent kingdom.

v.t: Wei emperor Cao Mao secretly plots with his attendants to kill Sima Zhao
v.s: Unwilling to remain a puppet, Cao Mao met with the Palace Attendant Wang Shen, Minister Wang Jing, and others to plan a surprise attack on Sima Zhao's mansion. Wang Shen and Wang Ye, however, informed Sima Zhao, forcing Cao Mao to act regardless.

v.t: Roman emperor Gallienus, busy with crises everywhere, neglects the east; Goths ravage the Balkans
v.s: Gallienus spent most of his reign fighting the Gallic Empire and barbarians. The Goths took advantage to invade the Balkans on a large scale, reaching the Aegean coast. The Roman army, with its forces dispersed, could not mount an effective counterattack.

v.t: Roman emperor Gallienus is assassinated by his officers at Milan; Claudius II succeeds
v.s: While besieging the rebel general Aureolus at Milan, Gallienus was killed by his own cavalry commander. Claudius II, an Illyrian, was proclaimed emperor by the army. Claudius quickly stabilized the situation and soon afterward decisively defeated the Goths, earning the title 'Gothicus' (Conqueror of the Goths).

v.t: After Cao Mao is killed, Sima Zhao installs Cao Huan as emperor
v.s: Cao Mao led several hundred retainers out of the palace to attack Sima Zhao. At the Southern Gate of the palace, Sima Zhao's subordinate Jia Chong ordered Cheng Ji to stab Cao Mao to death. To calm public outcry, Sima Zhao executed Cheng Ji and his three clans and, in the name of the Empress Dowager, demoted Cao Mao to commoner. Then he proclaimed Cao Huan (a grandson of Cao Cao) as emperor, Emperor Yuan of Wei. Sima Zhao now completely controlled the Cao Wei government.

v.t: Wei emperor Cao Mao leads troops against Sima Zhao and is killed at the Southern Gate
v.s: Unable to endure Sima Zhao's domination any longer, Cao Mao drew his sword, mounted his chariot, and led several hundred retainers to attack Sima Zhao's mansion. At the Southern Gate of the palace, Jia Chong ordered Cheng Ji to kill Cao Mao. This regicide shook the court and the country. To ease the backlash, Sima Zhao had Cheng Ji executed with his three clans. This was the most serious open confrontation before the Wei–Jin transition.

v.t: Sima Zhao plans to conquer Shu, sending Zhong Hui and Deng Ai to reconnoiter
v.s: After suppressing all internal opposition, Sima Zhao began planning the conquest of Shu Han. He appointed Zhong Hui as General Who Stabilizes the West, commanding the Guanzhong region, and together with Deng Ai and Zhuge Xu took charge of the western front. He ordered them to secretly train troops and map Shu Han territory in preparation for a large‑scale attack.

v.t: Odaenathus of Palmyra unites Syria and proclaims himself king
v.s: After defeating the Persian forces and recovering Mesopotamia, Odaenathus was given the title 'Commander of the East'. Although nominally subject to Rome, he was effectively independent, proclaiming himself king and making Palmyra his capital, establishing a semi‑independent kingdom within the framework of the Roman Empire.

v.t: Shu Han's Jiang Wei, to avoid the eunuch Huang Hao, goes to Tazhong to farm and avoid trouble
v.s: The Shu Han eunuch Huang Hao, who had seized power, resented Jiang Wei. After Jiang Wei's defeat in a northern campaign, he, wanting to avoid trouble, asked to be sent to Tazhong (modern Zhouqu, Gansu) to farm. There he opened up wasteland and stored grain, but by separating himself from the political center in Chengdu, he weakened Shu Han's border defenses.

v.t: Roman emperor Claudius II dies of illness; Aurelian succeeds, beginning the reunification of the empire
v.s: Claudius II died of illness after defeating the Goths. His cavalry commander Aurelian was proclaimed emperor by the army. Aurelian, an Illyrian officer of strong will, in his five‑year reign reconquered the Gallic Empire and the Palmyrene kingdom, reuniting the Roman Empire.

v.t: Jiang Wei's last northern expedition is defeated by Deng Ai; he never again undertakes a campaign
v.s: Jiang Wei led his army north, attacking Longxi. The Wei general Deng Ai set an ambush at Duan Valley (near modern Tianshui, Gansu) and heavily defeated Jiang Wei. The Shu army suffered heavy losses. Jiang Wei demoted himself and never again mounted a large‑scale northern expedition. This was his eleventh and last.

v.t: The last emperor of the Gallic Empire, Tetricus, consolidates his rule, but internal contradictions intensify
v.s: After the death of Postumus, his successor Tetricus was weak and ineffectual. Although the Gallic Empire remained independent, internal disaffection among the aristocracy and army set the stage for its easy absorption by Aurelian.

v.t: Jiang Wei pretends to surrender to Zhong Hui, plots to restore Shu, but the plot fails and he is killed
v.s: After Jiang Wei surrendered to the Wei general Zhong Hui, he saw that Zhong Hui had ambitions to rebel against Sima Zhao, so he persuaded Zhong Hui to hold Shu and declare independence. Zhong Hui imprisoned the Wei generals and claimed that he was acting under a posthumous edict from Empress Dowager Guo to overthrow Sima Zhao. But the Wei troops were unwilling to rebel; they attacked and killed Zhong Hui. Jiang Wei was killed in the melee. Any hope of restoring Shu Han was extinguished.

v.t: Wei attacks Shu; Deng Ai takes the Yinping shortcut, Zhong Hui attacks via the Xie Valley
v.s: Sima Zhao ordered Zhong Hui, Deng Ai, and Zhuge Xu to attack Shu in three columns. Zhong Hui tied down Jiang Wei at Jiange. Deng Ai took a force of elite troops over the Yinping shortcut, crossing snowy mountains, and surprised Jiangyou, then marched directly on Chengdu. The Shu defensive line collapsed.

v.t: Zhong Hui and Deng Ai fall out; Deng Ai is arrested, Zhong Hui rebels and is killed
v.s: After entering Chengdu, Deng Ai, without authorization, appointed local officials, arousing Sima Zhao's suspicion. Zhong Hui, jealous of Deng Ai's success, accused him of treason. Sima Zhao ordered Deng Ai's arrest, and Zhong Hui took command of all Wei forces. Zhong Hui then rebelled, but his own troops mutinied, killing both Zhong Hui and Jiang Wei. Deng Ai was also killed while being escorted back.

v.t: Deng Ai takes the Yinping shortcut and destroys Shu Han
v.s: The three Wei armies attacked Shu. Jiang Wei blocked Zhong Hui's main force at Jiange. Deng Ai led over 10,000 elite troops along the Yinping shortcut (modern Wen County, Gansu), climbing over mountains and cutting roads, reaching Jiangyou (modern Jiangyou, Sichuan). The Shu defender Ma Miao surrendered. Deng Ai then advanced to Mianzhu, where he killed Zhuge Zhan, and then pressed on to Chengdu. The Later Lord Liu Shan surrendered, and Shu Han fell.

v.t: For his achievement in conquering Shu, Sima Zhao is made King of Jin, granted the Nine Bestowals
v.s: For his success in conquering Shu, Emperor Yuan of Wei granted Sima Zhao the title of King of Jin, added territory, and allowed him to establish the Jin kingdom and its administrative organs. Sima Zhao now possessed all the regalia of an emperor, one step away from the throne.

v.t: Roman emperor Aurelian leads his army east to reunify the empire, defeating Palmyra
v.s: After stabilizing the Danube front, Aurelian marched east against the Palmyrene kingdom. He defeated the Palmyrene army near Antioch and then besieged Palmyra. Queen Zenobia tried to flee but was captured. The Palmyrene kingdom was destroyed, and the east was once again under Roman control.

v.t: Palmyra's ruler Odaenathus is assassinated by his cousin; his wife Zenobia becomes regent
v.s: Odaenathus was killed at a banquet by his cousin (or nephew). His young son Vaballathus succeeded, and his wife Zenobia became regent. Ambitious, Zenobia declared Palmyra's independence from Rome, conquered Egypt and much of Syria, and founded an independent Palmyrene Empire.

v.t: Sun Hao of Eastern Wu ascends the throne, known as the Last Emperor of Wu
v.s: Emperor Jing of Wu, Sun Xiu, died of illness. His son was young. The Chancellor Puyang Xing and the General of the Left Zhang Bu welcomed Sun Xiu's nephew Sun Hao as emperor. Initially, Sun Hao governed diligently, but he soon revealed his brutal nature, executing ministers and indulging in wine and women, causing Eastern Wu's national strength to decline rapidly.

v.t: Sima Zhao dies; his son Sima Yan succeeds as King of Jin, then forces the Wei emperor to abdicate, establishing the Western Jin
v.s: Sima Zhao died in 265, and his son Sima Yan succeeded as King of Jin. Imitating the story of Cao Pi usurping the Han, Sima Yan forced Emperor Yuan of Wei, Cao Huan, to abdicate, ascended the throne, proclaimed his dynasty as Jin, changed the era name to Taishi, and made Luoyang his capital. Cao Wei fell, and the Western Jin was established. Sima Yan posthumously honored Sima Yi as Emperor Xuan, Sima Shi as Emperor Jing, and Sima Zhao as Emperor Wen.

v.t: Sima Zhao dies; Sima Yan becomes King of Jin and begins planning the usurpation
v.s: After the conquest of Shu, Sima Zhao died of illness. His son Sima Yan succeeded to the title of King of Jin and the positions of Grand General and Chancellor. Sima Yan quickly arranged for Cao Huan to abdicate and formally proclaimed himself emperor at the end of the year.

v.t: Emperor Wu of Jin (Sima Yan) usurps the Wei throne and establishes the Western Jin
v.s: Sima Yan built an altar in the southern suburbs of Luoyang, received the abdication from Emperor Yuan of Wei, formally ascended the throne, named his dynasty Jin, changed the era name to Taishi, and enfeoffed Cao Huan as Prince of Chenliu. The establishment of the Western Jin marked the final phase of the Three Kingdoms period, with Eastern Wu remaining as the last independent regime.

v.t: Roman emperor Aurelian captures Palmyra, takes Zenobia prisoner, reunifying the east
v.s: Aurelian besieged Palmyra. Zenobia tried to escape but was captured. The city surrendered. Aurelian paraded Zenobia in golden chains through Rome, then granted her a country estate to live out her days. With this, the eastern part of the Roman Empire was once again unified under the central government.

v.t: Sima Yan enfeoffs twenty‑seven imperial clan members as kings, allowing them to maintain armies, planting the seeds of the War of the Eight Princes
v.s: To consolidate the Sima clan's rule, Emperor Wu of Jin enfeoffed twenty‑seven members of the Sima imperial house as kings, granting them fiefdoms and armies. The kings not only received revenues from their lands but also held military and civilian authority in their territories. This system became the institutional root of the later 'War of the Eight Princes'.

v.t: The Roman Senate elects Tacitus as emperor, but he soon dies
v.s: After Aurelian was assassinated, the Senate elected the elderly Claudius Tacitus as emperor. Tacitus led an expedition east to suppress a rebellion in Asia Minor, but soon died of illness.

v.t: Roman emperor Aurelian is assassinated by his staff while on campaign against the Sasanians
v.s: While preparing to campaign against the Sasanian Persians, Aurelian was killed by his private secretary in Thrace (modern European Turkey) for reasons unknown. In his five‑year reign, Aurelian had reunified the eastern and western halves of the fractured empire and was called 'Restitutor Orbis' (Restorer of the World). His death plunged Rome back into chaos.

v.t: Emperor Wu of Jin reorganizes internal administration, implements the household tax system, and cuts luxury expenses
v.s: After coming to power, Emperor Wu of Jin adopted a policy of thrift and good government, reducing court expenditures and forbidding the presentation of curios. He implemented the 'household tax system', under which peasants paid taxes according to their households: households of able‑bodied men annually paid three bolts of silk and three jin of floss silk. These policies somewhat restored the northern economy.

v.t: Roman emperor Probus is proclaimed emperor by the army and continues Aurelian's policies
v.s: After the death of Tacitus, the army proclaimed Probus as emperor. Probus, an Illyrian general, continued Aurelian's work of reunification, defeating barbarian invasions and beginning to restore border defenses.

v.t: Palmyra's king Odaenathus is assassinated; Zenobia takes power
v.s: Palmyra's ruler Odaenathus was killed at a banquet by his nephew (or cousin). His young son Vaballathus succeeded, and his wife Zenobia became de facto ruler. Ambitious, Zenobia immediately began expanding her territory, occupying Syria and Egypt.

v.t: Roman emperor Probus defeats the Germanic tribes invading Gaul, restoring the Rhine frontier
v.s: Probus led his army into Gaul, fought fierce battles against the Franks and Alamanni, and recaptured many cities occupied by barbarians. He repaired the Rhine forts and resettled some captured barbarians as Roman soldiers, setting a precedent for employing barbarians within the empire.

v.t: Western Jin cartographer Pei Xiu draws the 'Yu Gong Regional Map' and proposes the six principles of cartography
v.s: Pei Xiu, as Minister of Works in the Western Jin, oversaw the creation of the eighteen‑sheet 'Yu Gong Regional Map'. In his preface, he proposed the 'six principles of cartography': fenlü (scale), zhunwang (orientation), daoli (distance), gaoxia (relative height), fangxie (angle), and quzhi (curvature). These six principles laid the foundation for ancient Chinese cartography and were used until the Ming dynasty.

v.t: Small‑scale skirmishes break out between Western Jin and Eastern Wu on the Jingzhou border
v.s: After the Western Jin was established, there were frequent small‑scale border clashes with Eastern Wu in the Jingzhou region. The Jin general Yang Hu, stationed at Xiangyang, faced the Wu general Lu Kang. Each side had some victories, but Yang Hu pursued a policy of winning local hearts.

v.t: Sun Hao, Last Emperor of Wu, becomes more tyrannical, killing loyal ministers, making Eastern Wu politics even darker
v.s: After ascending the throne, Sun Hao initially restrained himself, but soon showed his cruel nature. He killed the officials who had enthroned him, Puyang Xing and Zhang Bu, and later several other imperial clansmen and high officials. He built great palaces, indulged in wine and women, and even invented 'water dungeons' to torture ministers. Eastern Wu's national strength collapsed, and it lost the people's hearts.

v.t: Roman emperor Claudius II (Gothicus) decisively defeats the Goths at Naissus
v.s: The Goths assembled tens of thousands of men to invade the Balkans. Claudius II led the Roman army to a decisive battle at Naissus (modern Niš, Serbia). Using a cavalry flank attack, the Romans killed over 30,000 Goths and captured a huge amount of booty. The Goths were driven back across the Danube and did not dare raid for several years. For this, the Senate granted Claudius II the title 'Gothicus'.

v.t: Roman emperor Probus clears remaining barbarians from Gaul and restores order
v.s: Probus continued to clean out the remaining Germanic tribes and rebels in Gaul, strengthening the border defenses. He encouraged vine‑growing in the border areas to develop the economy, enabling Gaul to slowly recover from the wars.

v.t: Roman emperor Aurelian (or Claudius II) reconquers the Gallic Empire
v.s: After defeating the Goths, Claudius II turned west and marched into Gaul. Tetricus, the last emperor of the Gallic Empire, was forced to surrender, and the Gallic Empire was destroyed. Roman control over Gaul, Britain, and Spain was restored.

v.t: Shapur I of Sasanian Persia dies; his son Hormizd I succeeds
v.s: Shapur I reigned for about thirty years and was the conqueror of the Sasanian dynasty. He defeated Rome, captured Valerian, and conquered the Kushans in the east, bringing the Sasanian Empire to its zenith. After his death, his son Hormizd I succeeded, but his reign was short.

v.t: The Xianbei Tufa tribe rebels in Liangzhou; Jin forces are sent but suffer defeat
v.s: Tufa Shujineng, a leader of the Hexi Xianbei (a branch of the Tuoba Xianbei), raised a revolt in Liangzhou (modern Wuwei, Gansu), killing the Jin inspector of Qinzhou, Hu Lie. Emperor Wu of Jin sent troops to suppress him, but they suffered repeated defeats, throwing Liangzhou into chaos. This revolt lasted several years and severely drained the resources of Western Jin.

v.t: Roman emperor Carus succeeds, declares war on Sasanian Persia, and restores Roman dominance in the east
v.s: Roman emperor Probus was killed by his soldiers, and Carus was proclaimed emperor. Carus led an army east, invaded Mesopotamia, and captured Ctesiphon. The Sasanian Persians sued for peace. Carus's victory temporarily restored Roman pre‑eminence in the east.

v.t: Egyptian Christian saint Catherine of Alexandria is executed for refusing to sacrifice to the Roman gods
v.s: According to Christian tradition, Catherine of Alexandria, a very learned Christian of Alexandria, was urged by the Roman emperor Maximinus (or Maximian) to sacrifice to the gods. She refused and was condemned to death on a spiked wheel. Later generations honored her as the patron saint of philosophers and scholars.

v.t: Roman emperor Aurelian begins construction of the Aurelian Walls to protect the city of Rome
v.s: In view of the fact that barbarians had reached the gates of Rome during the Crisis of the Third Century, Aurelian ordered the construction of a brick‑faced stone wall around Rome, about 19 kilometers long with 18 gates and hundreds of towers. The Aurelian Walls became the main defense of the city for more than a thousand years.

v.t: Roman emperor Probus is killed by his soldiers at Sirmium
v.s: During his reign, Probus enforced strict discipline, requiring soldiers to engage in agricultural labour and construction projects, which caused discontent. At Sirmium (modern Sremska Mitrovica, Serbia), a group of mutinous soldiers killed Probus. Carus then succeeded.

v.t: The war between Western Jin and the Xianbei Tufa Shujineng continues, with heavy Jin losses
v.s: Emperor Wu of Jin sent generals Su Yu and Yang Xin to attack Tufa Shujineng, but they were repeatedly defeated. Yang Xin was killed in battle. The Jin inspector of Liangzhou, Qian Hong, also suffered defeat. Tufa Shujineng gained control of the Hexi Corridor and threatened Guanzhong. Western Jin had to transfer troops from Guanzhong to suppress the rebellion.

v.t: Liu Shan, Later Lord of Shu Han, dies in Luoyang; posthumous title Duke Si
v.s: After being moved to Luoyang, Liu Shan was made Duke of Anle (the Happy Duke), with a fief of 10,000 households. He lived a comfortable life in Luoyang, giving rise to the phrase 'Happy here, I don't think of Shu.' Liu Shan died of illness in Luoyang, with the posthumous title Duke Si. The Western Jin buried him with ducal rites.

v.t: Queen Zenobia of Palmyra leads her army to occupy Egypt, cutting off Rome's grain supply
v.s: Zenobia sent her general Zabdas to invade the Roman province of Egypt. After defeating the Roman garrison, the Palmyrene army occupied Alexandria and all of Egypt. Zenobia declared herself Queen of Egypt and issued coins bearing her portrait. Rome's grain supply was severely threatened.

v.t: Jin general Yang Hu pursues a policy of conciliation in Jingzhou, maintaining peace on the border with Wu's Lu Kang
v.s: Yang Hu, as Governor of Jingzhou, faced Lu Kang of Eastern Wu. Yang Hu practiced benevolent policies: when fighting, he adhered to agreed times and did not launch surprise attacks; captured Wu soldiers' children were always repatriated. Lu Kang reciprocated, returning captured Jin soldiers, and the border remained peaceful. Yang Hu's intention was to erode the will of the Wu people and prepare for a future conquest.

v.t: Roman emperor Carus dies mysteriously during his eastern campaign; his son Numerian succeeds
v.s: Soon after his great victory at Ctesiphon, Carus died mysteriously in his camp (some said by lightning, others by assassination). His sons Numerian and Carinus succeeded as emperors of the east and west respectively. Rome again fell into internal conflict.

v.t: Roman emperor Aurelian captures Palmyra, completely conquering the east
v.s: Aurelian personally led a large army east, defeated the Palmyrene army near Antioch, and then besieged Palmyra. Zenobia tried to flee but was captured. The city surrendered. Aurelian paraded Zenobia in golden chains through Rome, then gave her an estate to live on. With this, the eastern provinces were all recovered for Rome.

v.t: Sun Hao, the tyrant of Eastern Wu, kills members of Lu Kang's family; Lu Kang, though angry, dares not speak out
v.s: Sun Hao, suspecting Lu Kang of colluding with the Western Jin, killed Lu Kang's brothers and many of his clan. Although Lu Kang still commanded the troops at Jiangling, his spirit was crushed, and he soon fell ill. The last famous general of Eastern Wu was suspected by the court, accelerating the fall of Wu.

v.t: Wang Jun, governor of Yizhou under Western Jin, begins building large warships in Shu, preparing to attack Wu
v.s: Following Yang Hu's advice, Emperor Wu of Jin appointed Wang Jun as governor of Yizhou to build large warships in Ba and Shu. Wang Jun built 'tower ships' (multi‑decked warships) that could carry over 2,000 men. He also equipped the ships with cranes and other naval weapons, preparing to sail downriver to conquer Wu.

v.t: Roman emperor Numerian dies; Diocletian is proclaimed emperor by the army, initiating Rome's revival
v.s: Numerian died (or was killed) on the march. His Praetorian prefect, Diocletian, was proclaimed emperor by the army. Diocletian quickly defeated his rival Carinus, reuniting the empire. He later carried out a series of reforms that ended the Crisis of the Third Century.

v.t: After Palmyra surrenders, the Roman army completely destroys the city
v.s: Palmyra rebelled again soon after its first surrender. Aurelian sent troops to suppress it, thoroughly destroying the city's fortifications and looting its wealth. Palmyra lost its independence forever. Zenobia was imprisoned in Rome.

v.t: Emperor Wu of Jin, seeking to fill his harem, orders a selection of beauties and forbids marriage throughout the realm
v.s: After unifying the north, Emperor Wu of Jin began to pursue pleasure. He ordered the selection of young women from throughout the country to fill his harem. During the selection, all marriages were forbidden. The number of imperial concubines reached several thousand; according to the Book of Jin, 'the harem had nearly ten thousand women.' This reflected the rapid corruption of the ruling class early in the Western Jin.

v.t: Roman emperor Diocletian introduces military and administrative reforms, strengthening central authority
v.s: After coming to power, Diocletian realized that the empire was too large for one man to rule. He began to plan the Tetrarchy (Four‑Emperor system), dividing the empire into east and west, each with an Augustus and a Caesar. At the same time, he increased the number of provinces from 47 to nearly 100 to reduce the power of provincial governors.

v.t: Diocletian reunifies the Roman Empire, ending the Crisis of the Third Century
v.s: Diocletian defeated Carinus and other usurpers, becoming sole ruler of the Roman Empire. He formally ended the fifty‑year period of military anarchy. Diocletian's rule brought temporary stability to the empire, but his Tetrarchy system would sow the seeds of future civil war.

v.t: The Roman Empire is reunified; after Aurelian recovers the Gallic Empire, the empire is again united under one ruler
v.s: Aurelian destroyed both the Palmyrene kingdom and the Gallic Empire, reuniting the Roman Empire's territory. The Senate conferred on him the title 'Restitutor Orbis' (Restorer of the World). This was the first time since the Crisis of the Third Century that the empire had been reunited.

v.t: Yang Hu presents his memorial 'On the Necessity of Attacking Wu', urging Emperor Wu of Jin to attack immediately
v.s: After many years in Jingzhou, Yang Hu thoroughly understood the internal weakness of Eastern Wu. He memorialized the emperor, pointing out that Sun Hao was tyrannical and the people longed for a change, making it the perfect time to destroy Wu. He proposed an attack on three fronts, from Yizhou, Jingzhou, and Yangzhou, by land and water. Emperor Wu hesitated, and Yang Hu soon died.

v.t: Roman emperor Aurelian is assassinated by his officers in Asia Minor
v.s: While preparing to campaign against the Sasanian Persians, Aurelian was killed in Thrace by his private secretary, allegedly because he had severely punished a slave who had lied, and the slave engineered the assassination. Aurelian's reign was brief, but he made important contributions to the empire's revival.

v.t: Diocletian appoints Maximian as Caesar to take charge of the western defense
v.s: Diocletian realized that the empire needed multiple rulers. He appointed his friend Maximian as Caesar (junior emperor) to be responsible for Gaul, Britain, and Spain. Maximian quickly suppressed the Bagaudae (peasant uprising) in Gaul, stabilizing the west.

v.t: Emperor Wu of Jin changes the era name to Xianning, symbolizing an era of peace
v.s: Emperor Wu of Jin issued an edict changing the era name to Xianning, meaning 'all attain peace'. At this time, Western Jin had unified the north for more than a decade, and the economy and society had somewhat recovered. But at court, the imperial clan lived in luxury, and the princes in their fiefs had large armies, posing latent dangers.

v.t: The Xianbei Tufa Shujineng continues his rebellion, capturing the major city of Liangzhou
v.s: Tufa Shujineng, allied with other Xianbei tribes, captured Guzang (modern Wuwei, Gansu), the capital of Liangzhou, and the Jin presence in the Hexi Corridor was on the verge of collapse. Emperor Wu of Jin sent generals Wen Yang and Ma Long to attack him.

v.t: Roman emperor Diocletian reforms the coinage, issuing new gold, silver, and bronze coins to stabilize the economy
v.s: To combat inflation, Diocletian issued new gold aurei and silver argentei of higher purity. But because the government lacked sufficient precious metals, the new coins quickly depreciated and did not solve the economic problems. Later, Diocletian issued the Edict on Maximum Prices, trying to control prices by government fiat.

v.t: Roman emperor Probus restores the Rhine and Danube frontiers, rebuilding border defenses
v.s: During his reign, Probus repeatedly defeated the Franks, Alamanni, and Goths, repairing the forts and walls destroyed by barbarians. He also relocated large numbers of conquered barbarians into Roman territory to cultivate wasteland.

v.t: Yang Hu, gravely ill, on his deathbed recommends Du Yu to succeed him and continue planning the conquest of Wu
v.s: Yang Hu, seriously ill, recommended Du Yu to succeed him as Governor of Jingzhou. He believed that Du Yu, accomplished in both civil and military affairs, could complete the task of conquering Wu. Du Yu, after taking office, continued Yang Hu's policy of winning hearts and actively prepared the army.

v.t: Diocletian leads an army to suppress a rebellion in Egypt, strengthening control over the eastern provinces
v.s: A revolt against Rome broke out in Egypt. Diocletian personally led an army to suppress it. He ruthlessly crushed the rebels, reorganized the Egyptian provinces, and reformed the tax system. Diocletian also visited Alexandria to discuss grain supplies with local officials.

v.t: The Roman army, under Emperor Probus, fully restores the Rhine frontier
v.s: Probus led his army to the Rhine, expelled the Franks and Alamanni who had occupied Roman territory, and rebuilt the forts and watchtowers that had fallen into disrepair after the collapse of the Gallic Empire. The Rhine frontier became stable again after decades.

v.t: Western Jin builds large numbers of warships in Yizhou and Jingzhou, training its navy to destroy Wu
v.s: Emperor Wu of Jin ordered Wang Jun to continue expanding the navy in Yizhou, building more tower ships, and ordered Du Yu to train the navy and build warships in Jingzhou. The Western Jin thus established two powerful fleets on the upper and middle Yangtze, preparing to sail downriver and wipe out Wu.

v.t: Du Yu carries out night raids in Jingzhou, capturing outposts on the Wu side of Jiangling
v.s: After taking over from Yang Hu, Du Yu immediately assumed an offensive posture. He launched night raids against the Wu outposts around Jiangling, seizing military supplies and weakening the Wu defenses. These raids paved the way for a future large‑scale river crossing.

v.t: Roman emperor Carus (or Aurelian/Probus) stabilizes the province of Dacia, repelling the Goths
v.s: The Roman army launched a successful offensive on the Danube front, clearing the Goths and other barbarians who had long occupied Dacia (modern Romania) and rebuilding the defensive system. Although Dacia had been abandoned during the Crisis of the Third Century, Rome temporarily restored its influence there.

v.t: Sasanian Persia and Rome clash again in Mesopotamia; neither side gains a decisive advantage
v.s: Sasanian king Bahram II became involved in border friction with Rome. The Roman army under Diocletian took a defensive posture, and no major war broke out.

v.t: Roman emperor Probus encourages vine‑growing on the frontiers to develop the economy
v.s: Probus issued an edict relaxing the ban on wine, allowing the cultivation of grapes and production of wine in Gaul, Pannonia, and other provinces. This stimulated economic recovery in the frontier regions and strengthened Roman control there.

v.t: Wang Jun, governor of Yizhou of Western Jin, completes construction of a huge fleet in Shu, ready to sail east to conquer Wu
v.s: The fleet that Wang Jun built in Yizhou was very large. The biggest tower ships were 120 paces long, could carry over 2,000 men, and had multiple decks with flags and wooden citadels. The fleet spread across the river. Emperor Wu decided to launch the final campaign the following year.

v.t: Western Jin launches a six‑pronged invasion of Wu, with over 200,000 men, by land and water, beginning the campaign to destroy Wu
v.s: Emperor Wu of Jin ordered a six‑pronged attack on Wu: Sima You (Grand General Who Guards the East) from Tuzhong; Wang Hun (General Who Pacifies the East) from Jiangxi; Wang Rong (General Who Builds the Army) from Wuchang; Hu Fen (General Who Pacifies the South) from Xiakou; Du Yu (Grand General Who Pacifies the South) from Jiangling; and Wang Jun and Tang Bin, with the Yizhou navy, sailing down the Yangtze. The total force exceeded 200,000, the largest ever assembled by Jin against Wu.

v.t: Ma Long leads 3,500 men west, puts down the Xianbei rebellion in Liangzhou
v.s: The Jin general Ma Long volunteered, recruited 3,500 elite soldiers, and led an expedition against Tufa Shujineng. Using a wheeled‑shield cart formation, he defeated the Xianbei forces and killed Tufa Shujineng, recovering Liangzhou. This successful suppression stabilized the northwest frontier.

v.t: Diocletian formally establishes the Tetrarchy, appointing Maximian as Augustus of the west
v.s: Diocletian formally put the Tetrarchy into effect. He appointed himself as Augustus of the East (residing at Nicomedia) and Maximian as Augustus of the West (residing at Milan). The two Augusti each appointed a Caesar as their deputies (Galerius and Constantius Chlorus). This system was designed to solve the problem of imperial defense, but it also laid the groundwork for future civil war.

v.t: After the conquest of Wu, Western Jin orders the reduction of provincial armies, simplifying the military establishment
v.s: Having unified the realm, Emperor Wu of Jin considered that the world was at peace and ordered the abolition of armies in the provinces and commanderies, leaving only a few permanent garrison troops. This weakened the central army of Western Jin and left local armed forces virtually nonexistent, paving the way for later barbarian invasions and the War of the Eight Princes.

v.t: Western Jin advances by land and water, captures Jianye, and ends Eastern Wu; the Three Kingdoms are finally unified
v.s: Wang Jun's navy sailed down from Yizhou, broke through the iron chains and stakes that Wu had placed across the Yangtze, and joined up with Du Yu and Wang Hun. The Wu Chancellor Zhang Ti led his troops to meet them but was defeated and killed. The Jin army reached Jianye. Sun Hao, imitating Liu Shan, tied his hands, carried a coffin, and surrendered. Eastern Wu was destroyed. The era of division that had begun with the Battle of Red Cliffs in 208 came to an end after 72 years of reunification. Emperor Wu of Jin granted Sun Hao the title of Marquis of Guiming.

v.t: After the unification of China, Western Jin enjoys the Taikang era of peace, with social and economic recovery
v.s: After destroying Wu, the Western Jin united the empire, and Emperor Wu changed the era name to Taikang. He put into effect a policy of rest, issuing the 'Ordinance on Land Allotment' and the 'Household Tax System', which set the amount of land to be cultivated by peasants and their tax rates. During the Taikang years (280–289), the economy and population grew, and the period was called the 'Taikang Prosperity' by historians. But the ruling class was increasingly corrupt, planting the seeds of future turmoil.

v.t: Sun Hao, the Last Emperor of Wu, dies in Luoyang; his title is Marquis of Guiming
v.s: In the second year of the Taikang era (281), Sun Hao, Last Emperor of Wu, died in Luoyang. After surrendering in 280, he was enfeoffed as Marquis of Guiming and moved to Luoyang. In Luoyang he acted respectfully, and Emperor Wu of Jin allowed him a comfortable life. He was buried with the rites of a marquis, and the Three Kingdoms period formally ended.

v.t: Roman emperor Probus suppresses the Bagaudae uprising in Gaul, restoring order
v.s: A large‑scale peasant and fugitive slave uprising called the Bagaudae broke out in Gaul. Probus led his army to suppress it, restoring order, and settled some of the rebels on frontier lands to farm. His strict discipline made him unpopular with the army.

v.t: The Jijun tomb in Western Jin yields a large cache of Warring States bamboo slips, including the 'Bamboo Annals'
v.s: A tomb robber opened the tomb of King Xiang of Wei (or King Anxi of Wei) of the Warring States period at Jijun (modern Ji County, Henan), discovering a large number of bamboo slips. Emperor Wu of Jin ordered Xun Xu, He Jiao, and others to collate the texts. They obtained dozens of cartloads, including the 'Bamboo Annals', 'The Legend of King Mu', the 'Book of Changes', and other precious ancient texts. These bamboo slips recorded history from the Xia, Shang, and Zhou down to the Warring States. The 'Bamboo Annals' has many passages differing from the received tradition and is of great historical value.

v.t: Carus's sons Carinus and Numerian fight for the throne; Roman civil war
v.s: After Carus's death, his sons Carinus and Numerian were proclaimed emperors by their respective armies. The two fought for control. Numerian soon died, and Carinus was ultimately defeated by Diocletian.

v.t: Diocletian consolidates his rule, maintaining peace with Sasanian Persia
v.s: After emerging victorious in the civil war, Diocletian became sole Roman emperor. He quickly concluded peace with Sasanian Persia and concentrated on internal reform and defense against the northern barbarians.

v.t: The Taikang prosperity continues to develop under Western Jin, with a growing economy and population
v.s: During the Taikang era, the Western Jin government encouraged farming, built water conservancy works, and reduced taxes. According to historical records, 'the realm was peaceful, taxes were fair, and the people were content with their work and happy in their professions.' This was also an active period for literature (Taikang literature), with writers such as Zuo Si, Lu Ji, Lu Yun, and Pan Yue.

v.t: Roman emperor Carus leads an army east against the Sasanian Persians, capturing Ctesiphon
v.s: Carus led the Roman legions east, defeated the Persian army in Mesopotamia, and captured the Sasanian capital Ctesiphon. The Persians sued for peace, and Rome achieved a major victory. But Carus died suddenly, and the Roman army withdrew.

v.t: Emperor Wu of Jin orders the princes to go to their fiefs, but many remain in the capital
v.s: To strengthen central control, Emperor Wu of Jin issued an edict ordering the princes to go to their fiefs. However, most remained in Luoyang and participated in government. The imperial princes had their own armies and staff, making them hard to control from the center.

v.t: Diocletian appoints Maximian as Augustus, formally establishing the Tetrarchy
v.s: Diocletian promoted Maximian to Augustus (senior emperor), and they jointly ruled the empire. Subsequently, he appointed Galerius and Constantius Chlorus as Caesars (junior emperors). The Tetrarchy system was now in operation.

v.t: Diocletian launches a comprehensive reform of the Roman Empire, including administration, military, and finances
v.s: Diocletian began a series of reforms: increasing the number of provinces from 47 to nearly 100 to strengthen local control; separating cavalry from the legions to form a mobile field army; reforming the tax system, taxing land and head; issuing new coinage and issuing the 'Edict on Maximum Prices'. These reforms restored stability to the empire but also strengthened absolutism and bureaucracy.

v.t: The Roman officer Diocletian is proclaimed emperor by his troops, ushering in a period of imperial revival
v.s: After the death of Numerian, his Praetorian prefect Diocletian was proclaimed emperor by the army in Asia Minor. He then defeated the western emperor Carinus, becoming sole ruler and ending the Crisis of the Third Century. Diocletian's reign is seen as the beginning of the 'Late Roman Empire', and his reforms prolonged the empire by another two centuries.

v.t: Western Jin continues its policy of rest and development, with a marked increase in the number of households
v.s: During the Taikang era, the Western Jin government carried out a nationwide census of households. The results showed a significant increase compared with the Three Kingdoms period. According to the Book of Jin, in the first year of Taikang (280) there were 2.45 million households, which by the third year (282) had risen to 3.77 million. This reflected the results of social stability and economic recovery after unification.

v.t: The Xianbei Murong Gui sends envoys to pay tribute to Western Jin and is appointed Xianbei governor
v.s: Murong Gui, leader of the Murong Xianbei (a branch of the Xianbei), sent envoys to Luoyang with tribute. Emperor Wu of Jin appointed him Xianbei governor, giving him authority over the western Liaoning region. This marked the beginning of a formal vassal relationship between the Murong Xianbei and Western Jin. Later the Murong clan grew increasingly powerful.

v.t: Diocletian is proclaimed Roman emperor by the army and establishes the Tetrarchy
v.s: After being proclaimed emperor by the army, Diocletian realized that the vast empire could not be governed by one man alone. He created the Tetrarchy, appointing Maximian as Augustus of the West, and Galerius and Constantius Chlorus as Caesars, to share defense and administrative tasks. This system temporarily stabilized the frontiers but collapsed soon after he abdicated.

v.t: Roman emperor Carus campaigns against the Sasanian Persians, winning a major victory
v.s: After ascending the throne, Carus led an army east against the Sasanian Persians, conquering Mesopotamia and threatening Ctesiphon. The Persian king Bahram II sued for peace, and Rome recovered control over Armenia and parts of Mesopotamia. This was one of the few major Roman victories over Persia in the late empire.

v.t: Under Murong Gui, the Murong Xianbei gradually grow strong and control western Liaoning
v.s: Using the title granted by Western Jin, Murong Gui consolidated the Xianbei tribes of western Liaoning, developed pastoral and agricultural production, attracted Chinese scholar‑gentry, and established a rudimentary administrative structure. The Murong clan became the foundation for the later Former Yan and Later Yan.

v.t: Diocletian begins comprehensive reforms of the Roman Empire, including administration, military, and economy
v.s: Having consolidated his rule, Diocletian embarked on large‑scale reforms. He divided the empire into eastern and western halves, each ruled by an Augustus and a Caesar (the Tetrarchy). He reorganized the army, strengthening the mobile field army. He also carried out a land and population census, establishing a new tax system.

v.t: Diocletian reforms the military, separating field armies from frontier forces
v.s: Diocletian divided the Roman army into frontier troops (Limitanei) and field armies (Comitatenses). The frontier troops garrisoned the borders, while the field armies were mobile reserves stationed in the interior, able to move quickly to any threatened front. This reform increased the army's responsiveness.

v.t: Western Jin aristocrats Shi Chong and Wang Kai compete in ostentatious luxury
v.s: Shi Chong, a rich man of Western Jin, competed with Wang Kai, the maternal uncle of Emperor Wu. Wang Kai washed his pots with syrup; Shi Chong used candles for fuel. Wang Kai set up a 40‑li screen of purple silk; Shi Chong used a 50‑li screen of brocade. When Wang Kai showed a coral tree given him by the emperor, Shi Chong smashed it and then produced dozens of even larger coral trees and let Wang Kai choose. The contest of wealth reflected the extreme corruption and extravagance of the Western Jin ruling class.

v.t: Maximian is appointed Augustus of the West by Diocletian
v.s: Diocletian formally promoted Maximian to be Augustus of the West, responsible for the defense of Gaul, Britain, Spain, and Africa. Maximian made Milan his capital. This established the western pillar of the Tetrarchy.

v.t: Roman commander Carausius proclaims himself emperor in Britain, establishing the Britannic Empire
v.s: The Roman naval commander Carausius was sent to clear pirates from the coasts of Gaul and Britain. He was accused of colluding with the pirates and embezzling booty. When Maximian ordered his execution, Carausius proclaimed himself emperor in Britain, establishing the Britannic Empire, and controlled Britain and the northern coast of Gaul, minting his own coins.

v.t: Roman general Constantius Chlorus leads a campaign to reconquer Britain, defeating Carausius
v.s: After Diocletian appointed Constantius Chlorus as Caesar, he was tasked with recovering Britain. He crossed the sea and defeated Carausius's army near Londinium (London), recovering southern Britain. Carausius was killed by his own officers.

v.t: Under Western Jin, official extravagance and bribery become rampant; government becomes corrupt
v.s: During the Taikang era, the high officials of Western Jin were extremely extravagant, competing with each other in luxury, and bribery was common. Ministers such as Wang Rong and He Jiao amassed great wealth. He Zeng spent 10,000 cash each day on meals and still 'had nowhere to lay his chopsticks'. Official corruption was serious, sowing the seeds for the short‑lived nature of the Western Jin.

v.t: Diocletian reforms the tax system, combining land and poll taxes
v.s: Diocletian introduced a new tax system: land (iugum) and head (caput) were used as units of assessment, and taxes (grain, oil, wine, etc.) were collected annually in kind. To ensure collection, he ordered a census of land and population every five years. This system increased the state's exploitation of peasants but provided a more stable revenue stream.

v.t: Emperor Wu of Jin indulges in pleasure, and government affairs decline
v.s: After unification, Emperor Wu of Jin became lazy, spending his time in the harem and often roaming the palace in a sheep cart. He favored his wife's relatives, the Yang clan, and pushed aside worthy ministers. Courtiers such as Shi Chong and Wang Kai vied in extravagance, and state affairs fell into neglect.

v.t: Small‑scale clashes continue between the Roman Empire and Sasanian Persia on the eastern frontier, no major war
v.s: Diocletian maintained peace with the Persian king Bahram II. Rome strengthened its Mesopotamian defenses, while Persia was preoccupied with incursions by the Kushans from the east. The border was relatively quiet.

v.t: The Xianbei Murong Gui moves his people to Tuhe (modern Jinzhou, Liaoning) to develop his power
v.s: Seeking more room to expand, Murong Gui moved his tribe from western Liaoning to Tuhe (modern Jinzhou, Liaoning). He made use of the area's proximity to the sea, developing fishing and salt, while also adopting Han farming techniques. His tribe grew rapidly.

v.t: Diocletian and Maximian meet in Milan to coordinate the Tetrarchy
v.s: Diocletian and Maximian met in Milan (Mediolanum) to discuss governance of the empire. They coordinated the dividing line between east and west and settled the order of succession. The meeting strengthened the stability of the Tetrarchy.

v.t: Emperor Wu of Jin enfeoffs the imperial princes as regional commanders with real military power, planting the seeds of civil war
v.s: In his later years, Emperor Wu of Jin appointed various princes as regional commanders, granting them substantial military and civilian authority. For example, Sima Liang was appointed overseer of Yuzhou, Sima Lun of Yong and Liang, Sima Dan of Qinzhou, etc. The princes now not only had fiefs but also controlled local armies, setting the stage for the later 'War of the Eight Princes'.

v.t: After the unification, Western Jin enjoys economic and cultural prosperity; Taikang literature flourishes
v.s: During the Taikang era, literature flourished. Zuo Si's 'Odes on the Three Capitals' caused 'Luoyang paper to become expensive'. Lu Ji, Lu Yun, Pan Yue, Zhang Hua, and others achieved great literary distinction. At the same time, scientific achievements of Western Jin are noteworthy, such as Pei Xiu's cartographic principles and Zhang Hua's compilation of the 'Treatise on Curious Things'.

v.t: Emperor Wu of Jin (Sima Yan) dies of illness, ending the Taikang prosperity
v.s: Emperor Wu of Jin died in Luoyang at the age of 55. His son Sima Zhong (Emperor Hui of Jin) succeeded, changing the era name to Yongxi. Emperor Hui was mentally deficient and incapable of ruling, so the consort clan member Yang Jun acted as regent. The death of Emperor Wu marked the turning point from prosperity to decline for Western Jin.

v.t: The consort clan member Yang Jun monopolizes the government, excluding the imperial princes
v.s: Yang Jun, the father‑in‑law of Emperor Wu, was appointed regent just before Emperor Wu's death. Yang Jun monopolized power and excluded the imperial prince Sima Liang and others. His arrogance and abuse of authority aroused widespread discontent.

v.t: Jia Nanfeng is installed as empress; later she will meddle in politics
v.s: Jia Nanfeng (daughter of Jia Chong) was formally installed as empress of Emperor Hui. Jia was jealous and ambitious. With Emperor Hui mentally deficient, she gradually began to interfere in state affairs and would become a central figure in the War of the Eight Princes.

v.t: First phase of the War of the Eight Princes: Empress Jia unites with Sima Liang and Sima Wei to eliminate Yang Jun, then turns on them
v.s: Empress Jia Nanfeng allied with Prince of Runan, Sima Liang, and Prince of Chu, Sima Wei, staged a coup, killed Yang Jun, and deposed Empress Dowager Yang. Then, using Sima Wei, she had Sima Liang killed, and finally had Sima Wei executed on a charge of forging imperial edicts. Empress Jia now monopolized power. The War of the Eight Princes erupted in full force.

v.t: Sima Liang and Wei Guan are recalled to act as regents, but Jia soon eliminates them
v.s: After Yang Jun was killed, Empress Jia recalled Prince of Runan Sima Liang and Grand Commandant Wei Guan to serve as regents. However, she soon distrusted them and used Prince of Chu Sima Wei to kill Sima Liang and Wei Guan. The fratricidal struggle among the Jin imperial princes had begun.

v.t: Zhang Hua becomes Minister of Works, maintaining relative stability under Empress Jia
v.s: Although Empress Jia monopolized power, she appointed the capable Zhang Hua and Pei Wei to manage government affairs. Zhang Hua, a commoner by origin and a learned man, with the empress's support, maintained relative political stability in the years 291–299.
