v.t: Empress Jia kills Sima Liang and Wei Guan, seizing full control of the government
v.s: Empress Jia used Sima Wei to kill Sima Liang and Wei Guan, then had Sima Wei executed on the charge of murdering senior ministers without authorization. She then ruled supreme, beginning nearly ten years of domination over the Western Jin court.

v.t: Diocletian strengthens the eastern frontier, building a series of forts
v.s: Diocletian toured the east and built a chain of forts and camps in Syria, Mesopotamia, and Arabia, known as the 'Diocletianic frontier'. These works effectively kept the Sasanian Persians at bay for the next half century.

v.t: Diocletian carries out administrative reforms, dividing provinces into smaller units, increasing the number to nearly 100
v.s: To strengthen central control, Diocletian greatly subdivided the 47 existing provinces into nearly 100. Above the provinces, he established twelve dioceses, managed by praetorian prefects. This reform weakened the power of provincial governors and increased centralization.

v.t: Empress Jia places her own people in key positions to consolidate power, but state affairs remain under Zhang Hua
v.s: Empress Jia used her influence to place her relatives, such as Jia Mi and Jia Wu, in important posts, excluding those she opposed. However, she retained confidence in Zhang Hua and Pei Wei, and they managed the government, allowing the Western Jin to maintain normal operation during the early years of the War of the Eight Princes.

v.t: The Tetrarchy is formally established: Diocletian and Maximian as Augusti, Galerius and Constantius Chlorus as Caesars
v.s: Diocletian formally divided the empire into four administrative regions: the East (Diocletian at Nicomedia), the West (Maximian at Milan), the Balkans (Galerius at Sirmium), and Gaul (Constantius Chlorus at Trier). The four emperors each had their own defense responsibilities, sharing the burden. This system temporarily stabilized the empire's borders.

v.t: The Xiongnu rebel Hao San rises in Shangdang, but is soon defeated
v.s: A Xiongnu named Hao San (possibly a precursor of Liu Yuan) raised a rebellion in Shangdang commandery (modern Changzhi, Shanxi), killing officials. The Jin army quickly suppressed it, and Hao San was killed. Though small in scale, this rebellion indicated that the Five Barbarian tribes were beginning to turn against Jin.

v.t: Diocletian suppresses a rebellion in southern Egypt, tightening control
v.s: A rebellion broke out in the southern part of Egypt (Upper Egypt). Diocletian led his army to suppress it, destroying the rebel strongholds and adjusting the administrative divisions of Egypt. He ceded some Nubian territory to the allied Nobatae in exchange for border security.

v.t: Diocletian reforms the coinage, issuing gold, silver, and bronze coins
v.s: Diocletian issued high‑purity gold (aureus) and silver (argenteus) coins, along with large numbers of bronze coins. But due to a shortage of precious metals, the new coins quickly depreciated and failed to control inflation.

v.t: A great fire breaks out in the Luoyang arsenal, burning a huge quantity of weapons and armor
v.s: The Jin arsenal in Luoyang caught fire, destroying 2.8 million weapons and pieces of armor, including high‑quality equipment from the time of Cao Cao and Cao Pi. This fire severely weakened the armament of the Western Jin central army.

v.t: Roman Empire goes to war with Sasanian Persia; Galerius is initially defeated
v.s: Caesar Galerius was ordered to attack the Sasanian Persians, but near Carrhae his army was ambushed and suffered heavy losses. Galerius barely escaped to Syria. Diocletian severely reprimanded him and ordered him to reform the army.

v.t: The Xianbei Yuwen tribe defeats the Murong tribe; Murong Gui flees to Liaodong
v.s: The Xianbei Yuwen tribe and the Murong tribe went to war. Murong Gui was defeated and fled with the remnants to Liaodong (modern Liaoyang, Liaoning), where he came under the protection of the Jin governor of Pingzhou. This was an early setback for the Murong clan.

v.t: Drought in Guanzhong leads to a large influx of refugees into Shu, causing social unrest
v.s: Severe drought and famine struck the Guanzhong and Longxi regions. Unable to survive, more than 100,000 refugees fled into Shu (modern Sichuan). There they clashed with local authorities, foreshadowing the later rebellion of Li Te.

v.t: Roman Caesar Galerius regroups his forces on the Persian frontier, preparing a counterattack
v.s: Galerius rebuilt his forces in Syria, recruiting large numbers of Illyrian and Gothic soldiers and receiving reinforcements from Diocletian. He used the winter to train his troops, planning a major offensive against Persia the following year.

v.t: The Di tribal leader Qi Wannian rises in Guanzhong and proclaims himself emperor
v.s: The Di chieftain Qi Wannian (in 296) raised a rebellion in the Jingyang and Fengyi area of Guanzhong, declaring himself emperor. The Jin court ordered Prince of Liang, Sima Tong, Xiahou Jun, Zhou Chu, and others to suppress him. Qi Wannian's forces quickly expanded, controlling a large part of central Shaanxi.

v.t: Roman Caesar Galerius attacks Persia again, initially defeated but then wins a great victory
v.s: Galerius led the Roman army in another attack on Persia. He was again defeated near Carrhae, but he quickly adjusted his tactics, using the mountainous terrain of Armenia to lure the Persians deep. Then at Satala, he routed the Persian king Narseh, capturing his family. The Persians sued for peace, and Rome gained northern Mesopotamia and parts of Armenia.

v.t: Diocletian issues the Edict on Maximum Prices, fixing maximum prices for goods and services
v.s: To control hyperinflation, Diocletian issued the Edict on Maximum Prices, setting maximum prices for thousands of goods and services, with the death penalty for violators. Since the decree ignored market forces, it was unenforceable and soon repealed.

v.t: Suppressing Qi Wannian's rebellion greatly increases Western Jin's military expenditure, depleting national strength
v.s: The Western Jin spent several years suppressing Qi Wannian's rebellion, consuming vast amounts of military supplies and money, while the refugee problem grew worse. The treasury was empty, the people lived in misery, and the state's strength declined sharply.

v.t: The Jin general Zhou Chu fights at the Battle of Liuwu without support and dies in combat
v.s: Prince of Liang, Sima Tong, commanded incompetently. He forced Zhou Chu to attack Qi Wannian's 70,000 men with only 5,000 troops. At Liuwu (modern Qian County, Shaanxi), Zhou Chu fought alone from dawn to dusk, killing tens of thousands of the enemy, but with his arrows and supplies exhausted and no reinforcements arriving, he was killed. The Jin army was heavily defeated.

v.t: Famine continues in Guanzhong; Li Te leads refugees into Shu to seek food
v.s: A great famine struck Guanzhong. The Di chieftain Li Te (father of Li Xiong) led tens of thousands of refugees through Hanzhong into Shu, seeking food. The governor of Yizhou, Zhao Xin, tried to use the refugees to strengthen his own power, and absorbed Li Te's followers. These refugees later formed the basis of the Cheng Han state.

v.t: Roman Caesar Galerius defeats the Sasanian Persian king Narseh in Armenia
v.s: Galerius led his army into Armenia and fought a decisive battle with the Persian king Narseh. Using a flank charge by his cavalry, the Romans routed the Persians, capturing Narseh's wife and children. Narseh sued for peace, and Rome gained northern Mesopotamia and five districts east of the Tigris. This was a great Roman victory over Persia.

v.t: Jin general Meng Guan leads elite troops to suppress Qi Wannian's rebellion, killing Qi Wannian
v.s: The Jin court changed its strategy, appointing Meng Guan as General Who Conquers the Rebels, and led elite troops to suppress Qi Wannian. At Mount Liang (modern Qian County, Shaanxi), Meng Guan fought a decisive battle and killed Qi Wannian, ending the rebellion in Guanzhong.

v.t: Social contradictions intensify in Western Jin; refugee uprisings erupt one after another
v.s: In the late Western Jin, land annexation was severe, taxes and corvée were heavy, and refugee uprisings broke out continuously. Besides Guanzhong and Shu, disturbances occurred in Jing, Yang, Xiang, and Jiao provinces. The foundations of Western Jin rule were shaken.

v.t: Diocletian's Edict on Maximum Prices has little effect
v.s: Diocletian's Edict on Maximum Prices, published in 301, had little effect because it ignored market forces. Merchants turned to black markets, and official markets languished. The edict was soon abandoned, and inflation continued.

v.t: Rome and Sasanian Persia sign a peace treaty, maintaining peace for nearly forty years
v.s: After Galerius's great victory, the Persian king Narseh sued for peace. The Treaty of Nisibis was signed: Rome gained five districts of northern Mesopotamia and sovereignty over western Armenia. Persia recognized Rome's gains. For the next forty years, Rome and Persia remained at peace.

v.t: Empress Jia deposes the crown prince Sima Yu, provoking widespread anger
v.s: Because the crown prince Sima Yu was not her son and she could not control him, Empress Jia framed him for treason, deposed him to commoner status, and imprisoned him in Jinyong Castle. This action enraged the imperial princes and ministers, giving Prince of Zhao, Sima Lun, a pretext to launch a coup.

v.t: Prince of Zhao, Sima Lun, kills crown prince Sima Yu, then stages a coup and kills Empress Jia
v.s: To seize power, Prince of Zhao Sima Lun (son of Sima Yi) first had Empress Jia kill the deposed crown prince Sima Yu, then, under the pretext of 'avenging the crown prince', led the palace guard in a coup, imprisoning Empress Jia and killing her associates. Sima Lun made himself Chancellor and monopolized power.

v.t: Roman general Constantius Chlorus defeats the usurper Allectus in Britain, recovering the province
v.s: Constantius Chlorus, appointed Caesar by Diocletian, was ordered to recover Britain. He crossed the sea, defeated the usurper Allectus, and restored Roman rule in Britain. For his military success, Constantius was given the nickname 'Chlorus' (the Pale).

v.t: Diocletian and Maximian hold a joint triumph in Rome, then plan to abdicate
v.s: Diocletian and Maximian celebrated a joint triumph in Rome, honoring their victories over the Persians and barbarians. Diocletian now began to consider abdication, hoping to establish a new succession system whereby both Augusti would abdicate at the same time and the Caesars would become Augusti.

v.t: In the Gupta Empire, the 'Tower of Sol' (or Surya Tower) is built
v.s: During the Gupta Empire (c.320–550), Indian astronomy and mathematics made great advances. The 'Tower of Sol' (probably an astronomical observatory at Ujjain) was a structure for observing the heavens, indicating Indian study of sundials and stars. The Gupta dynasty would shortly usher in the golden age of classical Indian civilization.

v.t: Prince of Zhao, Sima Lun, kills Empress Jia and her faction, seizes power; the War of the Eight Princes enters its second phase
v.s: Sima Lun staged a coup, deposed Empress Jia to commoner status and soon poisoned her. He killed her associates Zhang Hua and Pei Wei, made himself Chancellor, and took control of the government. The War of the Eight Princes now escalated from a palace coup to armed conflict among the princes.

v.t: The Kingdom of Aksum reaches its zenith under King Ezana
v.s: The Kingdom of Aksum (modern Ethiopia and Eritrea) reached its height under King Ezana. He conquered the Kingdom of Meroë (Kush), controlled Red Sea trade, minted gold coins, and converted to Christianity, making Aksum the first Christian kingdom in Africa. Aksum was considered one of the three great powers of the time, alongside Rome and Persia.

v.t: Polynesians settle Hawaii
v.s: Around 300–800 AD, Polynesian voyagers from the Marquesas Islands first discovered and settled the Hawaiian Islands. They used double‑hulled canoes and navigated by the stars across thousands of kilometers of ocean. A second wave of migration (c.1000–1200) from the Society Islands introduced a more complex class structure. Hawaiians developed sophisticated dryland and wetland taro irrigation systems, built large fishponds, and their population reached around 300,000 by the 18th century.
